legal_principle: Lord Langdale MR stated that for an express trust to be valid, three certainties must be present: certainty of words (intention), certainty of subject matter, and certainty of objects. If any of the three certainties is absent, no trust arises and the property passes outright to the donee. This is the foundational statement of the three certainties doctrine in equity.
key_quote: "In order to constitute a trust, three things are essential: first, the words must be imperative... secondly, the subject matter must be certain... and thirdly, the objects or persons intended to have the benefit of the trust must also be certain."
full_summary: Knight v Knight (1840) 3 Beav 148 is the foundational authority for the three certainties doctrine in English equity. Lord Langdale MR, sitting in the Court of Chancery, articulated the principle that every express private trust must satisfy three requirements before it will be recognised and enforced by equity. First, the words used by the alleged settlor must be sufficiently imperative to manifest an intention to create a legally binding obligation rather than a mere wish or hope — this is certainty of words or intention. Second, the subject matter of the trust must be identified with sufficient precision so that the trustee knows what property is to be held on trust and the beneficiaries know what they are entitled to claim — this is certainty of subject matter. Third, the objects or beneficiaries of the trust must be ascertainable with sufficient certainty for the trust to be enforceable — this is certainty of objects. The absence of any one of these three certainties is fatal to the creation of a valid express trust, and in that event the property will pass outright to the intended trustee as an absolute gift. The judgment has provided the organising framework for virtually all subsequent case law on express trust creation and is invariably the starting point for any analysis of the three certainties. It remains a central authority in both English and Irish equity jurisprudence.

legal_principle: The House of Lords held that the words 'in full confidence' in a will created a binding trust rather than a mere moral obligation, distinguishing earlier cases in which similar language had been held to create only a precatory request. The decision illustrates that the same words may be construed differently depending on context and that modern courts are more willing to treat precatory language as creating a trust where the testator's intent is clear.
key_quote: "In full confidence that she will act justly to our children" — construed as imposing a binding trust obligation on the donee.
full_summary: Comiskey v Bowring-Hanbury [1905] AC 84 is a House of Lords decision concerning the construction of precatory words in a will and their capacity to create a binding trust. The testator gave his wife his entire estate 'in full confidence that she will act justly to our children in dividing the same between them.' The question was whether these words imposed a legally binding trust obligation on the wife or merely expressed a hope. The House of Lords, by majority, held that the language, read in the context of the whole will, disclosed a clear intention to impose a trust on the wife to divide the residue among the children after her death. The decision is significant for two reasons. First, it demonstrates that the phrase 'in full confidence' is not inevitably precatory — context governs. Second, it draws a sharp contrast with Re Adams and the Kensington Vestry (1884), decided on nearly identical language, where the Court of Appeal reached the opposite conclusion. The divergence between the two cases underscores the highly fact-sensitive nature of the certainty of intention inquiry and the need to construe the entire instrument rather than isolated words. Comiskey is routinely cited in equity examinations as the counterpart authority to Re Adams.

case_name: Re Adams and the Kensington Vestry
court: England and Wales Court of Appeal
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal held that the words 'in full confidence that she will do what is right by my children' in a will did not create a trust but passed an absolute gift to the wife. The court found that the testator intended to rely on the wife's moral sense rather than impose a legal obligation, so no certainty of intention to create a trust was established. This case is the counterpart to Comiskey and illustrates how identical language can be construed differently depending on the full context of the instrument.
key_quote: "I give my wife... in full confidence that she will do what is right" — held to create no trust; absolute gift only.
full_summary: Re Adams and the Kensington Vestry (1884) 27 Ch D 394 is a landmark Court of Appeal decision on the certainty of intention requirement for express trusts and the treatment of precatory words. The testator left his real and personal estate to his wife 'in full confidence that she will do what is right by our children.' Cotton LJ and Lindley LJ, delivering the leading judgments, held that the words expressed merely a moral expectation rather than a legal obligation. The wife therefore took the estate absolutely. The court emphasised that the trend of earlier cases treating precatory words as creating trusts had been pushed too far, and that the court must look at the whole will to discern the testator's true intention. Where the natural reading of the instrument shows that the testator reposed confidence in the moral sense of the donee without intending to impose a legal constraint, no trust arises. The case is the classic counterpart to Comiskey v Bowring-Hanbury [1905], where nearly identical language was construed to create a trust. The contrast between the two decisions illustrates the highly contextual nature of the certainty of intention analysis and is a staple of equity examination questions. Students must be able to explain why the same phrase produced different outcomes in each case.

court: England and Wales Court of Appeal
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal held that the word 'desire' in a will was insufficient to create a binding trust, imposing only a moral obligation. Precatory words such as 'desire', 'wish', 'hope' and 'request' are generally treated as insufficiently imperative to satisfy certainty of intention, unless the overall context makes clear that a legal obligation was intended.
key_quote: "It is my desire" — held to be a precatory expression creating a moral obligation only, not a legally binding trust.
full_summary: Re Diggles (1888) 39 Ch D 253 is a Court of Appeal decision that forms part of the body of Victorian authority on precatory words and certainty of intention. The testator gave her estate to her daughter with the direction 'it is my desire that she allows my daughter an annuity of £25 per annum.' The Court of Appeal held unanimously that the word 'desire' was precatory — it expressed a wish rather than a command — and was therefore insufficient to impose a legally binding trust obligation on the daughter. The daughter took the estate absolutely. The case is one of a cluster of late-nineteenth century authorities establishing that certain categories of words — including 'desire', 'wish', 'hope', 'request', and 'recommend' — will generally be treated as precatory unless the surrounding context of the instrument supplies an imperative character that the words themselves lack. Re Diggles is typically studied alongside Re Adams and Lambe v Eames as authority for the proposition that equity will not now readily construe precatory language as creating a binding trust. It provides students with a concrete illustration of where the line is drawn between an imperative expression (sufficient for certainty of intention) and a mere precatory expression (insufficient). The case is also useful for illustrating the importance of examining the specific words used within their full documentary context.

legal_principle: The Privy Council held that words giving property to a wife 'feeling confident that she will act justly to our children in dividing the same when no longer required by her' were too uncertain to create a trust. The case is significant for its statement that uncertainty as to the subject matter of a trust will also tend to negate any apparent certainty of intention, as a trust cannot subsist over an uncertain subject.
key_quote: "Uncertainty in the subject of the gift has a reflex action upon the previous words, and throws doubt upon the intention of the testator."
full_summary: Mussoorie Bank Ltd v Raynor (1882) 7 App Cas 321 is a Privy Council decision on appeal from the High Court at Allahabad, and it contributes two distinct principles to the law of express trusts. First, it addresses certainty of intention, holding that the phrase 'feeling confident' was precatory and did not impose a binding obligation on the donee to divide the property among the children. Second, and perhaps more distinctively, the Board articulated the 'reflex action' principle: where the subject matter of an alleged trust is uncertain, that uncertainty reflects back on the earlier words and undermines any inference of a clear intention to create a trust. This is significant because it shows that the three certainties are not entirely independent — a defect in one certainty may cast doubt on the analysis of another. The case is also relevant to certainty of subject matter because the phrase 'what no longer required by her' made it impossible at the outset to know what property would remain. Mussoorie Bank is regularly cited in conjunction with Sprange v Barnard as an authority on both the precatory words problem and the subject matter uncertainty problem. The reflex action doctrine is a distinctive doctrinal contribution that students should be able to deploy in problem questions.

court: England and Wales Court of Appeal
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal held that informal words spoken by a man about a joint bank account were sufficient to establish certainty of intention to create a trust in favour of his partner. No technical language is required; it is sufficient that the settlor's words and conduct reveal an intention to hold property on trust for another. The case confirms that lay persons may create trusts without understanding the technical nature of the arrangement.
key_quote: "The money is as much yours as mine" — held sufficient to establish a trust by informal declaration.
full_summary: Paul v Constance [1977] 1 WLR 527 is a Court of Appeal decision that has become the leading authority for the proposition that certainty of intention can be established by informal language and conduct without the use of the word 'trust.' Mr Constance and Mrs Paul were cohabiting. Mr Constance received a payment of damages which he deposited in a bank account in his own name. He repeatedly told Mrs Paul that 'the money is as much yours as mine.' When Mr Constance died, the question arose whether Mrs Paul had a beneficial interest in the account. The Court of Appeal held that the cumulative effect of Mr Constance's words and conduct — including the repeated statements about joint ownership and the use of the account to bank joint bingo winnings — was sufficient to establish that he had constituted himself trustee of the account for himself and Mrs Paul in equal shares. The case establishes that equity does not demand technical language or an appreciation of the legal concept of a trust; what matters is that the owner of property manifested an intention to be under a legally binding obligation to hold it for another. Paul v Constance is essential reading for any question on certainty of intention and is frequently contrasted with cases involving more formal declarations in wills where the language was held insufficient.

court: England and Wales Court of Appeal (Chancery Division)
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal held that a direction to a wife to dispose of property at her death 'in any way she thinks best for the benefit of' their children was insufficiently imperative to create a trust; the words merely expressed a wish and created an absolute gift. The case is a classic illustration of the precatory words problem and confirms that expressions of hope or wish, unaccompanied by imperative language, do not satisfy certainty of intention.
key_quote: "For the use and benefit of my family in any way she thinks best" — held precatory, not imperative; no trust created.
full_summary: Lambe v Eames (1871) 6 Ch App 597 is a Court of Appeal decision from the Chancery Division that is regularly cited as one of the foundational authorities on precatory words and certainty of intention. The testator bequeathed his estate to his wife 'to be at her disposal in any way she thinks best for the benefit of herself and her family.' The Court held that these words did not create a trust in favour of the family. James LJ delivered the leading judgment, explicitly criticising earlier decisions that had too readily treated expressions of hope and confidence as creating binding trusts: 'in creating cases of this kind a court of equity has been haunted by the fear of making a will inoperative.' He indicated that the court should no longer be so quick to find a trust from precatory language. The wife therefore took absolutely. Lambe v Eames is significant for signalling a judicial retreat from the earlier trend of finding trusts in precatory words, a trend that was further confirmed in Re Adams and the Kensington Vestry. The case is useful to students for illustrating the shift in judicial attitude towards precatory words over the latter half of the nineteenth century and for demonstrating that the word 'benefit' does not in itself supply the requisite imperative character.

legal_principle: The court held that where an absolute gift is followed by words purporting to create a trust, but the trust is void for uncertainty, the donee takes absolutely. If the purported trust fails for any reason, including uncertainty of objects, the gift will be treated as absolute rather than resulting back to the settlor. This principle — sometimes called the Lassence v Tierney rule — remains an important part of the law on failed trusts.
key_quote: "If there is an absolute gift... and no valid trust is created, the donee takes free of any trust."
full_summary: Lassence v Tierney (1849) 1 Mac & G 551 is a Court of Chancery decision that established an important rule about the consequence of a failed trust where the underlying gift was absolute. The case involved a gift under a will that purported to give property outright to a donee while attaching directions that, if valid, would have constrained that gift by imposing trust obligations. The court found that the trust element was invalid — whether for uncertainty or another defect — and held that the donee was entitled to take the property absolutely. The principle established is that where property is given absolutely and a superimposed trust fails, the initial absolute gift stands and the donee is not obliged to hold on resulting trust for the settlor's estate. This rule must be distinguished from the situation where the gift itself is made conditional upon the trust operating, in which case failure of the trust might cause the property to result back. Lassence v Tierney is relevant to questions on certainty of intention and certainty of objects because it governs what happens when a trust fails, and students are expected to identify whether, on given facts, the Lassence rule applies or whether a resulting trust arises instead. The case should be read alongside Re Wilson [1913] for the contrasting resulting trust outcome.

legal_principle: The Irish High Court applied the certainty of intention doctrine in the context of an alleged oral trust, confirming that Irish equity follows the English approach in requiring clear evidence of an intention to create a legally binding trust obligation. Precatory language unsupported by clear evidence of legal intent will not satisfy the first certainty.
key_quote: "A trust requires a clear indication of the settlor's intention to create a legally enforceable obligation, not merely a hope or expectation."
full_summary: Tierney v Tough [1914] 1 IR 142 is an Irish High Court decision that confirms the reception of the English certainty of intention doctrine into Irish equity. The case arose from an alleged oral trust, with the plaintiff contending that the defendant had declared himself trustee of certain property. The court applied the established English principles — drawing on Knight v Knight and the precatory words line of cases — and held that no trust arose because the language and circumstances fell short of the clear manifestation of an intention to impose a legally binding obligation. The decision is significant for Irish equity students for several reasons. First, it demonstrates that Irish courts adopted the English three certainties framework without modification. Second, it addresses the particular evidential difficulties that arise when a trust is alleged to have been created orally rather than by written instrument, noting that the court must be especially careful to distinguish a genuine trust declaration from a family arrangement founded on moral expectation. Third, it illustrates that the precatory words doctrine applies not only to wills but to any alleged declaration of trust. Tierney v Tough is the principal Irish authority on certainty of intention and is an essential case for students studying equity in an Irish law programme.

legal_principle: The court held that a bequest to a husband of money 'for his sole use' with the remainder 'at his death... to give... to the remaining part of my relations' was void for uncertainty of both intention and subject matter. The expression 'remaining part' was held too uncertain as it was unknowable at the time of the bequest what would remain. This case is frequently cited alongside Palmer v Simmonds as an early authority on certainty of subject matter.
key_quote: "The remaining part of what is left" — too uncertain to form the subject matter of a trust.
full_summary: Sprange v Barnard (1789) 2 Bro CC 585 is an eighteenth-century Court of Chancery decision that is one of the earliest authorities on the combined requirements of certainty of intention and certainty of subject matter. The testatrix left money to her husband 'for his sole use' and directed that 'the remaining part of what is left' at his death should go to named relations. The question was whether a valid trust existed over the 'remaining part.' The court held that no trust could arise. The phrase 'remaining part of what is left' was inherently uncertain — it was impossible at the date of the will to know what, if anything, would remain after the husband's use of the fund during his lifetime. This uncertainty as to subject matter, combined with the ambiguity of the testator's intention, was fatal to the trust. The husband therefore took the fund absolutely. Sprange v Barnard is frequently cited alongside Palmer v Simmonds (1854) and Mussoorie Bank v Raynor (1882) as part of the trilogy of subject matter uncertainty cases. It is also relevant to the question of whether an absolute gift can co-exist with a superimposed trust obligation, and connects to the Lassence v Tierney principle. Students should note the interaction between the two certainties that are in issue in this case.

legal_principle: The court held that a direction to a legatee to 'hold [the brooch] in trust for your children and their issue' created a valid trust. The judgment is significant for its examination of how courts approach certainty of intention when imperative language such as 'in trust' is actually used — the use of trust language creates a strong presumption of an intent to create a trust.
key_quote: "I desire my said daughter to hold the said brooch... in trust" — the words 'in trust' are imperative and create a valid trust obligation.
full_summary: Re Steele's Will Trusts [1948] Ch 603 is a Chancery Division decision that occupies an interesting position in the certainty of intention case law. Unlike the majority of the cases in this area — which are concerned with whether precatory language is sufficiently imperative to create a trust — Re Steele's Will Trusts involves the use of explicit trust language: the testatrix directed her daughter to 'hold the said brooch in trust for your children and their issue.' Wynn-Parry J held that the trust was valid, following an earlier decision in which similar language in similar circumstances had been enforced. The case is significant for confirming that where the words 'in trust' are actually employed, there is a strong — though not irrebuttable — presumption that the person using them intended to create a legally binding trust obligation. It also raises interesting issues about the extent to which a court will follow an earlier decision on identical or near-identical will language, and about the interaction between certainty of intention and certainty of objects (which was also briefly considered). Re Steele's Will Trusts provides a useful contrast for students studying the precatory words cases: whereas those cases deal with language that stops short of using the word 'trust', this case illustrates what happens when the draftsman does use that word and the consequences that follow.

legal_principle: The court held that a direction to hold 'the bulk' of a fund on trust was void for uncertainty of subject matter. The word 'bulk' is insufficiently precise to identify a determinate portion of the estate. The certainty of subject matter requirement demands that the subject matter of the trust be identified with sufficient certainty for the court to enforce it and for the trustees to know what they hold.
key_quote: "The bulk of my residuary estate" — the word 'bulk' does not identify a sufficiently certain proportion and the trust fails.
full_summary: Palmer v Simmonds (1854) 2 Drew 221 is the leading authority on the certainty of subject matter requirement and is invariably cited as the starting point for any discussion of that certainty. The testatrix directed that 'the bulk of my residuary estate' should be held on trust. Kindersley V-C held that the word 'bulk' was insufficiently certain to identify the subject matter of the trust. 'Bulk' might connote the greater part, the largest portion, or some other fraction of the estate, but it does not specify a precise quantum. Because the trustees could not know with certainty what property they were required to hold on trust, the purported trust failed for want of certainty of subject matter. The testatrix's residuary estate therefore passed as on intestacy. Palmer v Simmonds establishes the foundational principle that the property comprising the trust fund must be identifiable with precision — not merely identifiable in a general or impressionistic way. The case is the anchor authority against which all subsequent subject matter cases are measured. Students should be able to explain why 'bulk' is insufficient while 'reasonable income' (Re Golay) is sufficient, and should be able to apply the Palmer v Simmonds standard to novel fact patterns involving imprecise quantifiers. The case also pairs naturally with Sprange v Barnard (1789) as an early authority on this certainty.

legal_principle: The court held that a direction to pay a 'reasonable income' to the beneficiary was sufficiently certain, as 'reasonable income' is an objective standard the court can determine. This case qualifies the strictness of the certainty of subject matter requirement by recognising that where an objective yardstick exists for determining the quantum, the trust will not fail for uncertainty.
key_quote: "A reasonable income" — held sufficiently certain as the court can objectively ascertain what is reasonable in the circumstances.
full_summary: Re Golay's Will Trusts [1965] 1 WLR 969 is a Chancery Division decision that introduces an important qualification to the certainty of subject matter requirement. The testator directed his executors to allow a named beneficiary to enjoy 'a reasonable income' from his properties during her lifetime. Ungoed-Thomas J held that this direction was valid and enforceable, notwithstanding the apparent vagueness of the phrase 'reasonable income.' The crucial distinction drawn by the court is between uncertainty that is conceptual (where no objective standard exists to resolve the ambiguity) and uncertainty that is merely quantitative but capable of resolution by reference to an objective standard. 'Reasonable income' is sufficiently certain because a court can determine, by reference to the beneficiary's circumstances, lifestyle, and the income available from the estate, what constitutes a reasonable income. The case is frequently contrasted with Palmer v Simmonds (where 'bulk' provides no objective yardstick) and is cited as authority for the proposition that the certainty requirement is not satisfied only by arithmetically precise descriptions — an objective standard of reasonableness will suffice. Re Golay is an important case for problem questions in which students are asked to assess whether a trust fails for uncertainty of subject matter, as it provides the primary exception to the strict precision requirement.

court: England and Wales Court of Appeal
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal held that a declaration of trust over 50 shares out of a larger holding of 950 identical shares was sufficiently certain, as the shares were identical and fungible. Unlike Re London Wine Co, where physical segregation of tangible goods was required, with intangible fungible assets such as shares no segregation is necessary. The decision has been criticised for inconsistency with the tangible goods line of cases but remains good law.
key_quote: "A trust of 50 unspecified shares out of 950 identical shares is sufficiently certain as the shares are indistinguishable from one another."
full_summary: Hunter v Moss [1994] 1 WLR 452 is a Court of Appeal decision that addresses the certainty of subject matter requirement as it applies to intangible fungible assets, particularly shares. The defendant employer held 950 shares in a company and orally declared that he held 50 of those shares on trust for the plaintiff employee. No specific shares were identified or segregated. The Court of Appeal held that the declaration of trust was valid. Dillon LJ reasoned that because all 950 shares were identical — each carrying the same rights and indistinguishable from the others — there was no need to segregate 50 specific shares in order to constitute a valid trust over them. This distinguishes the position from Re London Wine Co and Re Goldcorp, where the goods were tangible and physical segregation was required to identify the specific items subject to the trust. Hunter v Moss has attracted significant academic criticism on the grounds that it creates an unprincipled distinction between tangible and intangible assets. Critics argue that the same uncertainty that defeats a trust over an unappropriated portion of a wine collection should equally defeat a trust over an unidentified portion of a share holding. Nevertheless, the decision is binding in England and has been applied and followed in subsequent cases. It is essential reading for any question on certainty of subject matter and fungible assets.

legal_principle: The court held that where specific bottles of wine had not been physically segregated and set aside for individual purchasers, no trust in favour of those purchasers could arise. Certainty of subject matter requires that, for tangible goods, the specific items subject to the trust must be identified and separated from the general mass; an unappropriated portion of a bulk of goods cannot form the subject matter of a trust.
key_quote: "There can be no trust of a portion of a bulk of tangible goods unless the specific goods subject to the trust have been physically segregated and appropriated."
full_summary: Re London Wine Co (Shippers) Ltd [1986] PCC 121 is a Chancery Division decision that establishes the physical segregation requirement for tangible goods in the context of certainty of subject matter. Customers of the company had purchased specific quantities of wine, but the company had not set aside and labelled individual customers' bottles from its general warehouse stock. When the company became insolvent, the customers argued that they held beneficial interests in the wine under a trust. Oliver J rejected this argument. The customers could not point to any specific bottles that were subject to a trust in their favour — the wine was held in an undifferentiated bulk and no appropriation had occurred. Because the specific goods forming the subject matter of any alleged trust were unascertained, certainty of subject matter was not satisfied. The customers were therefore merely unsecured creditors of the company's insolvent estate. Re London Wine is the cornerstone of the principle that physical segregation is required for tangible goods, and it is regularly contrasted with Hunter v Moss to illustrate the different treatment of tangible and intangible assets. The case has important practical implications in commercial insolvency contexts and is a standard examination authority on certainty of subject matter.

legal_principle: The Privy Council confirmed that no proprietary trust could arise in favour of customers who had purchased gold bullion from a company that had not physically segregated their purchased gold from its general stock. No title passed and the customers were merely unsecured creditors on insolvency. The case reinforces the London Wine principle that physical segregation is required for tangible goods to form the subject matter of a trust.
key_quote: "The absence of appropriation of specific goods to individual customers prevents any proprietary interest from arising; the customers are unsecured creditors only."
full_summary: Re Goldcorp Exchange Ltd [1995] 1 AC 74 is a Privy Council decision on appeal from New Zealand that consolidates and confirms the physical segregation requirement established in Re London Wine Co. The company sold gold bullion to customers, promising to hold their purchased gold in storage. In practice, the company maintained only a general stock of bullion without separating individual customers' purchases. When the company became insolvent, customers claimed proprietary interests in the general bullion stock. The Privy Council, in a judgment delivered by Lord Mustill, held that no trust or other proprietary interest could arise in favour of the customers because no appropriation of specific bullion to specific customers had taken place. Without physical segregation and appropriation of the tangible goods, the subject matter of any alleged trust was insufficiently certain. The customers were unsecured creditors and could not claim priority over secured creditors in the insolvency. Re Goldcorp is significant for confirming that the London Wine principle applies across different categories of tangible goods and in cross-jurisdictional contexts. It also contains important analysis of the distinction between legal title, equitable title, and contractual rights in the context of commercial sales, and is a leading case on both certainty of subject matter and proprietary claims in insolvency. It must be read alongside Re London Wine and Hunter v Moss as part of the core subject matter certainty trilogy.

court: England and Wales Court of Appeal
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal held that tenancy deposits held in a general bank account could form the subject matter of a trust in favour of tenants, as money is fungible and the lack of physical segregation does not prevent certainty of subject matter with respect to intangible monetary claims. The case supports the proposition that fungible intangibles do not require segregation to satisfy certainty of subject matter.
key_quote: "Deposits of money in a general account are sufficiently certain to form the subject matter of a trust without need for physical segregation."
full_summary: Re Chelsea Cloisters Ltd (1980) 41 P&CR 98 is a Court of Appeal decision concerning tenancy deposits paid to a landlord company and held in a general bank account alongside the company's own funds. When the company became insolvent, the question arose whether the deposits were held on trust for the tenants, which would give the tenants a proprietary interest superior to the claims of general creditors. The Court of Appeal held that the deposits were indeed held on trust. The fact that the deposit monies had been commingled in a general account did not prevent certainty of subject matter, because money — as an intangible, fungible asset — does not require physical segregation in the same way that tangible goods do. The court was able to trace each tenant's deposit as a defined sum within the account. Re Chelsea Cloisters predates Hunter v Moss [1994] by fourteen years but anticipates the same reasoning: intangible fungible assets (whether money or shares) do not require physical segregation to satisfy the certainty of subject matter requirement. The case is an important practical authority on the treatment of commingled deposits in an insolvency context and provides support for the more controversial Hunter v Moss decision. It also illustrates the interaction between certainty of subject matter and the law of tracing.

court: England and Wales Court of Appeal
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal established the 'complete list' test for fixed trusts: the trustee must be able to compile a complete list of all beneficiaries before the trust can be enforced. If it is impossible to ascertain the identity of every beneficiary, the trust fails for uncertainty of objects. This contrasts with the 'is or is not' test for discretionary trusts established in McPhail v Doulton.
key_quote: "A trust for equal distribution among a class requires the court to be able to compile a complete list of all beneficiaries; if this is impossible, the trust fails."
full_summary: IRC v Broadway Cottages Trust [1955] Ch 20 is the leading Court of Appeal authority on the certainty of objects requirement for fixed trusts, and more specifically on what became known as the 'complete list' or 'list certainty' test. The trust in question purported to benefit a wide class of persons described in terms that made it impossible to compile an exhaustive list of all potential beneficiaries. The Court of Appeal held that a fixed trust — one that requires the trustee to distribute equally among all members of a defined class — cannot be administered unless the trustee can identify every member of the class. If it is impossible to compile such a complete list, the trust fails for uncertainty of objects. The rationale is practical: a court supervising the administration of a fixed trust must be able to ensure that each beneficiary receives the correct proportionate share, which is impossible if the total number of beneficiaries cannot be ascertained. The Broadway Cottages test was subsequently overruled by the House of Lords in McPhail v Doulton [1971] with respect to discretionary trusts, but it remains the applicable standard for fixed trusts. Students must be able to distinguish between the two tests and identify which applies on given facts.

legal_principle: The House of Lords overruled the Broadway Cottages complete list test for discretionary trusts and held that the correct test is whether it can be said with certainty of any given individual that they 'is or is not' a member of the class. A discretionary trust does not require a complete list of all potential beneficiaries; it is sufficient that the concept is sufficiently certain to allow any individual to be definitively included or excluded. This is the leading case on certainty of objects for discretionary trusts.
key_quote: "A discretionary trust does not fail for uncertainty of objects if it can be said with certainty of any given individual whether that person is or is not within the class of beneficiaries."
full_summary: McPhail v Doulton [1971] AC 424 (also known as Re Baden's Deed Trusts No 1) is the most important House of Lords decision on certainty of objects for discretionary trusts and represents a fundamental shift in the law. Prior to this decision, IRC v Broadway Cottages Trust had established that the complete list test applied to all trusts, including discretionary trusts. The House of Lords, by a majority of three to two, overruled Broadway Cottages with respect to discretionary trusts and held that the correct test is the same as that applicable to powers of appointment (drawn from Re Gulbenkian's Settlements): whether it can be said with certainty of any given individual whether that person 'is or is not' within the class of beneficiaries. Lord Wilberforce delivered the leading judgment, reasoning that a discretionary trust is not defeated merely because a complete list of all potential objects cannot be compiled — the trustee's duty is to survey the class and exercise a genuine discretion, not to distribute equally. The reformulation of the test was of enormous practical importance, enabling broadly drafted employee benefit trusts and family discretionary trusts to remain valid. McPhail v Doulton is essential for any equity examination question on certainty of objects, and students must be able to apply the 'is or is not' test and to contrast it with the complete list test for fixed trusts.

legal_principle: The House of Lords established that the 'is or is not' test applies to powers of appointment: a power will only be valid if it can be said with certainty of any individual that they 'is or is not' within the class. The case paved the way for extending the same test to discretionary trusts in McPhail v Doulton. A fiduciary power is invalid if the class is so uncertain that the donee cannot know who is a possible object.
key_quote: "A power of appointment is valid only if it can be said of any given individual whether that person falls within or without the class of persons in whose favour it could be exercised."
full_summary: Re Gulbenkian's Settlements [1970] AC 508 is a House of Lords decision decided one year before McPhail v Doulton and forming the immediate doctrinal precursor to that landmark case. The settlement in question contained a power of appointment exercisable in favour of a widely defined class. The House of Lords, in a judgment delivered by Lord Upjohn, held that a power of appointment — as distinct from a fixed trust — does not require the donee to compile a complete list of all possible objects. Instead, a power is valid if, in relation to any individual claimant, it can be said with certainty whether that individual is or is not within the class. This 'is or is not' formulation was adopted directly by Lord Wilberforce in McPhail v Doulton and applied to discretionary trusts. Re Gulbenkian is therefore an essential stepping stone in the development of the modern certainty of objects doctrine. The distinction between fixed trusts (requiring a complete list), powers (requiring only the 'is or is not' test), and discretionary trusts (now also subject to the 'is or is not' test after McPhail) is central to equity and trusts examination questions. Students must be able to explain the progression from Broadway Cottages through Gulbenkian to McPhail as a coherent doctrinal narrative.

legal_principle: The court held that a wide power of appointment was valid even though the class was very large, as a power does not require the trustee to distribute equally among all objects and can be exercised in favour of any member of the class. The case is an early authority on the distinction between fixed trusts and powers, and foreshadows the McPhail test.
key_quote: "A power of appointment is valid if it can be exercised in favour of any member of the described class, even if the class is very large and not fully enumerable."
full_summary: Re Gestetner Settlement [1953] Ch 672 is a Chancery Division decision that is historically significant as an early recognition of the distinction between the certainty requirements applicable to fixed trusts on the one hand and powers of appointment on the other. The settlement conferred a power of appointment exercisable in favour of a broadly described class of persons connected with the settlor's business and family. The class was very large and it was not possible to compile a complete list of all potential objects. Harman J held that the power was valid nonetheless, reasoning that a power is fundamentally different from a fixed trust: the holder of a power is not required to distribute among all objects but merely has a discretion to appoint in favour of any one or more of them. Accordingly, the complete list test that applies to fixed trusts (later confirmed in IRC v Broadway Cottages Trust [1955]) does not apply to powers. Re Gestetner Settlement anticipates the reasoning later adopted by the House of Lords in Re Gulbenkian's Settlements [1970] and McPhail v Doulton [1971], and is useful for tracing the intellectual history of the 'is or is not' test. It is studied primarily as a precursor to those landmark decisions and for its analysis of the trust/power distinction in the context of certainty of objects.

legal_principle: The court considered whether a discretionary trust for 'relatives' of the testator was sufficiently certain as to objects, and found that 'relatives' could be given a workable meaning by reference to blood relations. The case is frequently cited in connection with the evidential uncertainty problem — the distinction between conceptual uncertainty (void) and evidential uncertainty (curable) in the context of certainty of objects.
key_quote: "The word 'relatives' is not so conceptually uncertain as to defeat a trust; it can be given a workable meaning by reference to blood relations."
full_summary: Re Brown [1954] Ch 39 is a Chancery Division decision that contributes to the law on certainty of objects through its analysis of the word 'relatives' as a descriptor of a class of beneficiaries. The question before the court was whether a discretionary trust for the testator's 'relatives' was void for uncertainty of objects. The court held that 'relatives' could be given a sufficiently certain meaning — by reference to blood relations traceable through legitimate descent — to survive the certainty challenge. The decision is significant because it draws attention to an important distinction in the certainty of objects analysis: the distinction between conceptual uncertainty and evidential uncertainty. Conceptual uncertainty arises where a descriptive term is so vague that it has no ascertainable core meaning; such uncertainty is fatal and cannot be remedied by evidence. Evidential uncertainty arises where the concept is clear enough but it is difficult in practice to determine whether a particular individual satisfies the description; such uncertainty does not in itself defeat a trust, though it may give rise to administrative difficulties. Re Brown illustrates that 'relatives', while potentially a wide class, is not conceptually uncertain. The case is studied alongside Re Baden's Deed Trusts No 2 [1973] for its contribution to the analysis of class descriptions in the context of the McPhail test.

court: England and Wales Court of Appeal
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal held that where a trust fails for uncertainty of objects (or any other reason), the property results back to the settlor or their estate by way of resulting trust. The case affirms that a failed express trust does not leave the trustee to benefit personally — equity intervenes to return the property to the person who provided it.
key_quote: "Where a trust fails, the trustee holds on resulting trust for the settlor or the settlor's estate; the trustee cannot take beneficially."
full_summary: Re Wilson [1913] 1 Ch 314 is a Court of Appeal decision on the consequences of a failed express trust, specifically addressing what happens to property when a trust fails for uncertainty of objects. The court affirmed the foundational equitable principle that equity does not permit a trustee to take a beneficial interest in property that was transferred to them for the purpose of holding it on trust for others. Where that trust fails — for whatever reason, including uncertainty of objects, uncertainty of subject matter, or failure to comply with formality requirements — the trustee holds the property on resulting trust for the settlor or, if the settlor is deceased, for the settlor's estate. The resulting trust arises by operation of law and cannot be excluded by the express terms of the failed instrument. Re Wilson is an important bookend case for the three certainties topic: having established what happens when all three certainties are present and a valid trust arises, students must also understand what happens when one or more certainties is absent. Re Wilson provides the answer for most cases of failed trusts — a resulting trust back to the settlor — while Lassence v Tierney provides the answer for the specific case where the underlying gift was absolute. Students should be able to identify which principle applies in any given set of examination facts.

court: England and Wales Court of Appeal (Chancery Division)
legal_principle: Turner LJ established the fundamental rule that equity will not perfect an imperfect gift. For a trust to be completely constituted, the legal title to the trust property must be transferred to the trustee by the appropriate mode of transfer for that type of property. If the settlor fails to use the appropriate mode of transfer, the gift is incomplete and the intended transferee cannot enforce it in equity. 'There is no equity in this court to perfect an imperfect gift.'
key_quote: There is no equity in this court to perfect an imperfect gift.
full_summary: Milroy v Lord is the foundational authority on the constitution of trusts and perfection of gifts in English equity. The case concerned an attempt by a Mr Milroy to transfer shares in a bank to Samuel Lord, to be held on trust for the plaintiff. The purported transfer was executed by way of a power of attorney, but the shares were never formally transferred into Lord's name in the company's register. Turner LJ, delivering the leading judgment in the Court of Appeal in Chancery, articulated the governing principle with clarity: for a gift to be effective, the donor must do everything necessary according to the nature of the property to transfer it to the donee. Three modes of making a gift were identified: an outright transfer, a transfer to trustees, or a self-declaration of trust. If the donor fails to adopt the correct mode, the gift is imperfect and equity will not supply the deficiency. The phrase 'equity will not assist a volunteer' encapsulates this rule. The case has been refined by later decisions such as Re Rose and Pennington v Waine, but remains the starting point for any analysis of constitution of trusts. In Irish law, the same principle applies: an imperfect gift cannot be completed by equity, and the court will not treat an incomplete transfer as a declaration of trust unless there is clear evidence of such an intention.
topics: equity will not assist a volunteer

court: England and Wales Court of Appeal
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal held that an imperfect transfer of shares could be treated as complete in equity where it would be unconscionable for the donor to resile from the gift. The court found that the donor had authorised registration and that the donee had been allowed to act as director in reliance on the gift; it would be unconscionable to permit the donor to renege. This case represents a significant qualification to the strict Milroy v Lord rule.
key_quote: Where it would be unconscionable for the donor to resile from the gift, equity will treat the imperfect gift as complete even without a formal transfer.
full_summary: Pennington v Waine is a significant and controversial Court of Appeal decision that substantially qualifies the strict rule in Milroy v Lord. Ada Pennington wished to transfer shares in a family company to her nephew Harold Waine. She signed a share transfer form and gave it to her agent, who placed it on the company file without registering it. Harold was also appointed a director in reliance on the intended gift. Ada died before the transfer was registered. The Court of Appeal held that the gift was effective in equity, even though the formal registration had not occurred. Clarke LJ, concurring with Arden LJ, reasoned that it would be unconscionable for Ada's estate to resile from the gift because Harold had acted in reliance on it by becoming a director. Arden LJ approached the matter by asking at what point it became unconscionable for the donor to withdraw, concluding that this occurred when Ada authorised her agent to deal with the transfer. The decision has been criticised for its uncertainty and for stretching the Re Rose principle beyond its proper bounds, but it remains good law in England and Wales. It introduces an unconscionability-based exception to Milroy v Lord that sits uneasily alongside the traditional rules on constitution of trusts.

legal_principle: Jessel MR held that where a donor fails to make an effective inter vivos gift of property but appoints the intended donee as executor of their will, the vesting of the legal title in the donee as executor completes the gift, provided the donor had a continuing intention to make the gift up to the moment of death. This rule — the Strong v Bird exception — operates as a method of perfecting an imperfect gift through operation of law.
key_quote: The appointment of the intended donee as executor operates to vest the legal title in them, completing the gift where the donor maintained a continuing intention.
full_summary: Strong v Bird establishes one of the recognised exceptions to the rule that equity will not perfect an imperfect gift. Bird lodged with Mrs Strong and paid rent. Mrs Strong agreed to release him from a debt of 900 pounds. She subsequently forgot about the release and continued to deduct it from rent payments for a period, but Jessel MR found that she had maintained a continuing intention to release the debt. When Mrs Strong died, Bird was appointed her executor, and the question arose whether legal title to the chose in action (the debt release) vested in him as executor. Jessel MR held that the appointment of Bird as executor vested in him the legal ownership of the chose in action, thereby completing what had been an imperfect gift. Two requirements must be satisfied: first, the donor must have manifested an intention to make an immediate gift at the outset; second, the donor must have maintained that intention without abandonment until death. The rule in Strong v Bird has been extended by subsequent decisions, including Re James (1935), which applied it to administrators. The rule has a narrower scope than Re Rose, as it depends on the coincidence of the donee being appointed as personal representative.

legal_principle: The court extended the Strong v Bird rule to administrators as well as executors, holding that where the intended donee is appointed administrator of the donor's estate, the vesting of legal title in the administrator completes the imperfect gift. The case confirms that the Strong v Bird exception is not confined to executors appointed by will and may apply to any personal representative in whom legal title becomes vested.
key_quote: The vesting of legal title in an administrator of the estate of the donor operates in the same way as vesting in an executor to complete an imperfect gift under the Strong v Bird principle.
full_summary: Re James is the principal authority for the proposition that the rule in Strong v Bird extends beyond executors to administrators. The case involved a situation where the donor had made an imperfect gift of property during their lifetime, and the intended donee was subsequently appointed administrator of the donor's estate (rather than executor under a will). Farwell J held that there was no principled distinction between an executor and an administrator for the purposes of the Strong v Bird rule: in both cases, legal title to the property of the deceased vests in the personal representative. Accordingly, the same perfecting effect arises. The decision was not without controversy. Critics argued that where a person dies intestate and an administrator is appointed, the appointment is not the deliberate act of the deceased expressing an intention to benefit the donee-administrator, as it is where a testator chooses to appoint a particular person as executor. Despite this criticism, Re James remains good law and is regularly cited as extending the ambit of the Strong v Bird rule. The case reinforces that the policy behind the rule — preventing the strict rules of transfer from defeating an honest and continuing donative intention — applies equally to all forms of personal representation.

legal_principle: The court applied the Strong v Bird rule where a husband made an imperfect gift to his wife during his lifetime and she subsequently became his executrix. The court found a continuing intention to benefit the wife and held that the vesting of the estate's assets in her as executrix completed the gift. The case is a classic application of the Strong v Bird rule to a spousal gift.
key_quote: The continuing intention of the donor to make the gift, combined with the vesting of title in the donee as personal representative, perfects the gift.
full_summary: Re Stewart is a straightforward application of the Strong v Bird principle in a spousal context. A husband sought during his lifetime to make a gift of certain property to his wife, but the gift was never completely constituted because the appropriate mode of transfer was not employed. The husband died, and his wife was appointed as his executrix. Neville J held that the vesting of title in the wife as executrix operated to perfect the imperfect gift, provided the husband had maintained a continuing intention to make the gift up to his death. The court was satisfied on the evidence that the husband's intention had remained constant. The case is notable for illustrating how the Strong v Bird rule operates in practice: the gift does not arise from any new act at the time of death, but is treated as having been perfected by the operation of law consequent on the donee's appointment as personal representative. Re Stewart also confirms that the rule is not limited to commercial or financial assets but can apply to any property capable of vesting in a personal representative. It remains a useful illustrative authority alongside Strong v Bird itself, Re James, and Re Gonin.

legal_principle: The court held that the Strong v Bird rule did not apply where the alleged gift was of a house (which would have required a formal conveyance), and where the evidence of a continuing intention to make the gift was insufficient. The case illustrates the limits of Strong v Bird: it only operates where the donor maintained an unequivocal continuing intention to make the gift from its inception to death, and the appropriate mode of transfer must be capable of being effected by the appointment.
key_quote: The Strong v Bird rule requires clear evidence of a continuing and unequivocal intention to make the gift up to the moment of death; equivocal evidence of intention will not suffice.
full_summary: Re Gonin is a cautionary authority on the limits of the Strong v Bird rule. The deceased had allegedly promised to give her daughter the family home during her lifetime, but the promise was never implemented by way of formal transfer or conveyance. When the deceased died, her daughter sought to argue that her appointment as administratrix perfected the imperfect gift under the Strong v Bird rule. Walton J rejected the claim on two independent grounds. First, the evidence of a continuing donative intention was equivocal: the deceased had at times indicated an intention to give the house to her daughter but at other times had acted inconsistently with that intention. Strong v Bird requires an unequivocal and continuous intention maintained from the initial promise to the date of death. Second, and more fundamentally, Walton J doubted whether the rule could apply to real property at all, at least after 1925, since a conveyance of land requires specific formalities that cannot be displaced merely by the appointment of the donee as personal representative. Re Gonin thus places important restrictions on the scope of the Strong v Bird exception and reminds practitioners that the rule is not a general tool for validating imperfect gifts.

case_name: T Choithram International SA v Pagarani
legal_principle: The Privy Council held that where a donor declared himself a trustee as well as being one of several trustees, he could not claim that the trust was incompletely constituted because he had not transferred the property to all the trustees. A donor who declares himself trustee cannot subsequently claim to be a volunteer and rely on the incompleteness of the trust. The case is an important qualification of Milroy v Lord in the context of self-declaration by one of several trustees.
key_quote: Where the donor is also one of the trustees, the donor cannot assert that the gift is incompletely constituted; he holds the property subject to the trust from the moment of declaration.
full_summary: T Choithram International SA v Pagarani (Choithram) is a Privy Council decision on appeal from the British Virgin Islands that represents a significant and pragmatic qualification of the Milroy v Lord rule. Thakurdas Choithram Pagarani (TCP), an elderly and very wealthy man, executed a foundation deed and declared publicly that he was giving all his wealth to the foundation. He was one of the foundation's trustees. He died before formally transferring his assets to all the trustees. His estate argued that the gift was incompletely constituted. The Privy Council, in a judgment delivered by Lord Browne-Wilkinson, held that TCP could not resile from his declaration. Because TCP was himself one of the trustees, he held the property from the moment of his declaration subject to the trust obligations. To allow him or his estate to renege would be unconscionable. The Privy Council distinguished Milroy v Lord on the basis that it is impossible for a person who has declared himself to be a trustee to be a volunteer vis-a-vis himself. The decision reflects a growing willingness in equity to look beyond formalism and prevent unconscionable conduct, and it anticipates the approach taken in Pennington v Waine.

court: England and Wales Court of Appeal
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal held that where a donor has done everything in their power to transfer property to the donee and all that remains is a ministerial act by a third party (such as company registration), equity will treat the transfer as complete from the moment the donor did everything necessary. This is the Re Rose principle — equity treats a gift as complete when the donor has done everything they are able to do, even if formal completion by a third party remains outstanding.
key_quote: Once the donor has done everything in his power to transfer the property, equity will treat the transfer as complete, even if ministerial acts by a third party remain outstanding.
full_summary: Re Rose is one of the most important cases on the constitution of trusts and is frequently examined. Eric Rose executed two transfers of shares in a private company: one as an outright gift and one as a transfer to trustees. The transfers were not registered by the company until after the critical date for estate duty purposes. The question was whether the transfers were effective in equity at the earlier date when Rose had signed and delivered the transfer forms. The Court of Appeal held that they were. Once the donor had executed and delivered the transfer forms and done everything within his own power to transfer the shares, the equitable interest passed to the donee, even though the legal title could not pass until registration by the company. The key principle, known as the Re Rose principle, is that a court of equity treats the transfer as complete from the moment the donor has done all that he personally is required to do. The principle was refined and applied in Mascall v Mascall (1984) and Pennington v Waine (2002). It is distinguished from the Milroy v Lord rule by the crucial fact that the outstanding steps in Re Rose were to be performed by a third party, not the donor.

legal_principle: The High Court of Australia applied and refined the Re Rose principle, holding that a purported transfer of a joint tenancy interest was incomplete because a formal memorandum of transfer had not been executed. The court confirmed that a donor has not done 'everything necessary' until they have executed all documents required for the legal transfer to occur. The case qualifies the Re Rose principle by emphasising the need for the donor to have taken all steps within their personal power.
key_quote: A donor has not done everything necessary to complete a gift unless they have executed all documentation that they personally are required to sign to effect the transfer.
full_summary: Corin v Patton is an important Australian authority that applies and qualifies the English Re Rose principle. The case arose from a dispute over the effect of a purported transfer of a joint tenancy interest in Torrens title land. Mrs Patton executed a memorandum of transfer in favour of her husband, but the transfer had not been registered and a duplicate certificate of title had not been produced before her death. Her estate argued that the transfer was incomplete and that she therefore had not done everything necessary. The High Court of Australia (Mason CJ, Brennan and Deane JJ in the majority) agreed that the transfer was incomplete, but the court took the opportunity to refine the Re Rose principle. The majority held that the donor has done 'everything necessary' when they have taken every step that the law requires of them personally to effect the transfer; if any further step must be personally taken by the donor, the gift is not yet complete. This is a stricter formulation than some readings of Re Rose and clarifies that the principle does not extend to situations where the donor still has personal obligations outstanding. Corin v Patton is regularly cited in Australian and New Zealand equity courses and provides a useful comparative perspective on constitution of trusts.

legal_principle: The House of Lords held that a transfer of shares by Mr Vandervell to the Royal College of Surgeons was completely constituted, as all necessary steps had been taken to transfer legal title. However, the retention of an option to repurchase the shares by a company nominated by Vandervell gave rise to a resulting trust of the option in favour of Vandervell, generating an unexpected tax liability. The case is important for both constitution of trusts and resulting trust doctrine.
key_quote: A completely constituted transfer passes the legal estate; but where the beneficial interest of an option is not disposed of, a resulting trust arises in favour of the transferor.
full_summary: Vandervell v IRC is a landmark House of Lords decision that spans both constitution of trusts and resulting trust law. Guy Vandervell wished to fund a chair at the Royal College of Surgeons and arranged for his bank to transfer shares in his company to the College. The College would receive dividends for a period and then, pursuant to an option held by Vandervell's trustee company, the shares would be repurchased. The House of Lords held that the transfer of the shares to the College was completely constituted: legal title had passed and the gift was effective. However, the option to repurchase the shares was held by Vandervell's nominee company on a resulting trust for Vandervell himself, because Vandervell had not disposed of his beneficial interest in the option. This meant that dividends paid on the shares during the relevant period were attributable to Vandervell for surtax purposes, creating a massive and entirely unintended tax liability. The Inland Revenue successfully assessed Vandervell to tax. The case is frequently cited as an example of the dangers of incomplete tax planning and of the automatic nature of resulting trusts. It must be read alongside Re Vandervell's Trusts (No 2) [1974], in which the Court of Appeal held that Vandervell had subsequently divested himself of the resulting trust by exercising the option.

legal_principle: The Irish High Court applied the established principles of constitution of trusts in an Irish context, confirming that an alleged oral declaration of trust over land could not be enforced unless either the declaration satisfied the requirements of formality under the Statute of Frauds 1677 or the trust arose by operation of law. The case illustrates the practical importance of formalities requirements in Irish trust law.
key_quote: An oral declaration of trust over land is unenforceable unless it satisfies the statutory requirements of formality or arises by operation of law.
full_summary: Doyle v Byrne is an Irish High Court decision that applies the fundamental principles of equity and trusts law to an Irish land dispute. The case arose from an alleged oral agreement or declaration of trust concerning land, and the plaintiff sought to enforce that declaration in equity. The court considered whether the oral declaration was sufficient to create an enforceable express trust. Applying the Statute of Frauds 1677 (then applicable in Ireland), the court held that a declaration of trust in respect of land must be evidenced in writing signed by the party able to declare such trust. An oral declaration, however clearly proved, does not satisfy the statutory requirement. The court further confirmed that this rule does not apply to trusts arising by operation of law, such as resulting and constructive trusts, which may be established without written evidence. The case is a useful illustration of the interaction between the common law formalities rules and equitable principles in Irish law, and it confirms that Irish courts apply the same doctrinal approach to constitution of trusts as English courts, modified only where Irish statute or case law has diverged. The decision is cited in Irish trust law textbooks as an early example of the Irish courts' fidelity to English equity principles in the post-independence period.

legal_principle: The House of Lords confirmed that half-secret trusts are enforceable in equity provided the trust terms were communicated to and accepted by the trustee before or at the time of execution of the will. Communication after the will is ineffective for a half-secret trust (unlike a fully secret trust where later communication may be permissible). Equity enforces the trust to prevent the statute (Wills Act) being used as an instrument of fraud.
key_quote: A testator cannot reserve to himself a power of making future unwitnessed dispositions by merely naming a trustee; the trust must be communicated and accepted before or at the time of executing the will.
full_summary: Blackwell v Blackwell is the principal House of Lords authority on half-secret trusts and is an essential case in any study of testamentary equity. The testator bequeathed a sum of money to five trustees 'for the purposes indicated by me to them', to apply the income 'for the purposes and in the manner indicated'. Before executing the will, he had communicated the objects of the trust to the trustees, who accepted the obligation. The testator's widow argued that the trust was invalid on the ground that the purposes had not been set out in the will in accordance with the Wills Act 1837 and were therefore unenforceable. The House of Lords, disagreeing with a line of earlier authority, held that the half-secret trust was valid and enforceable. The equitable doctrine of secret trusts operates dehors the will — that is, outside the will — and is not rendered invalid by the Wills Act, because equity will not allow a statute to be used as an instrument of fraud. Viscount Sumner gave the leading speech, emphasising that the doctrine is grounded in the prevention of unconscionable conduct by a trustee who has accepted a trust obligation. Crucially, the House also confirmed the rule that for half-secret trusts, the communication must occur before or contemporaneously with the execution of the will: unlike fully secret trusts, later communication is ineffective. Re Keen subsequently applied this principle.

court: England and Wales Court of Appeal
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal held that communication of the terms of a half-secret trust by handing the trustee a sealed envelope before the will was executed was effective communication, even though the envelope was not opened until death. The rule in Blackwell requires communication before or at the time of the will; the sealed letter handed before the will satisfies this requirement. However, communication in a letter written after the will is executed is too late.
key_quote: Communication of the terms of a half-secret trust by sealed letter before execution of the will is effective; communication after the will is executed is not.
full_summary: Re Keen is the leading Court of Appeal authority on the mechanics of communication in half-secret trust cases. The testator's will left a sum to trustees 'to be held upon trust and disposed of by them among such person, persons or charities as may be notified by me to them during my lifetime'. Before making the will, the testator had handed one of the trustees a sealed envelope containing the name of his mistress, whom he wished to benefit. The trustees accepted the obligation. After the testator's death, the question arose whether the trust was enforceable. The Court of Appeal held that the sealed letter handed before the will constituted valid communication within the Blackwell v Blackwell rule: the fact that the envelope was sealed and not read until death did not prevent it from being communication in advance of the will. The envelope was in the possession of the trustee before execution and was thus a valid prior communication of the trust terms. However, the court noted an inconsistency: the will referred to future notifications, suggesting communication might be permissible after the will, which would be contrary to the Blackwell rule for half-secret trusts. Because of this inconsistency, the trust ultimately failed on other grounds. Re Keen thus illustrates both what constitutes effective communication and the dangers of a will that contradicts the terms of a prior communication.

legal_principle: The court held that where the testator leaves property to a person apparently absolutely but intends to impose a secret trust, communication of the trust to the legatee is essential. Without communication, the legatee takes free of any trust, as there is no fraud in allowing them to keep property they never promised to hold on trust for another. Only where the legatee accepted the trust obligation will equity intervene to enforce it.
key_quote: A secret trust cannot be enforced against a legatee who never communicated with the testator and never accepted any trust obligation; there is no fraud to prevent.
full_summary: Wallgrave v Tebbs is a foundational case on the requirement of communication and acceptance in fully secret trusts. The testator left money to two individuals, Tebbs and Martin, on the face of the will absolutely and without condition. After his death, documents were found indicating that the testator had intended the property to be applied for charitable purposes. However, neither Tebbs nor Martin had been told of this intention during the testator's lifetime and had not accepted any trust obligation. Vice-Chancellor Wood held that the secret trust could not be enforced. The equitable doctrine of secret trusts is grounded entirely in the prevention of fraud: where a person accepts property on the strength of a promise to hold it on trust for another, it would be unconscionable to allow them to keep it free of that obligation. But where no communication has occurred and no promise has been made, there is no fraud. The legatee takes the property in accordance with the will and is not affected by undisclosed intentions of the testator. Wallgrave v Tebbs thus establishes the foundational principle that communication and acceptance by the trustee before or at the time of execution of the will (or, for fully secret trusts, before death) is indispensable. The case is commonly read alongside McCormick v Grogan (1869) on the fraud basis of secret trust enforcement.

legal_principle: The House of Lords confirmed that the basis of enforcing a fully secret trust is the prevention of fraud: it would be unconscionable to allow a legatee who accepted property on the strength of a promise to hold it for a third party to keep it for themselves. The equity acts not on the will but on the conscience of the secret trustee. Lord Hatherley stated that the court requires evidence of the clearest and most indisputable character before it will disturb a will on the basis of an alleged secret trust.
key_quote: The court will not permit the statute to be used as a cloak for fraud; where a legatee accepted property on a promise to hold it for another, the legatee is bound by that promise.
full_summary: McCormick v Grogan is the leading House of Lords decision on the theoretical basis for the enforcement of fully secret trusts. The testator wrote a letter to Grogan shortly before his death, indicating that he had left all his property to Grogan by his will and giving him a list of persons he wished to benefit. The letter made clear that Grogan was to use his own judgment about what, if anything, to pay to the named persons. Grogan withheld payment from McCormick, who sued. The House of Lords held that no trust had been created because the testator had deliberately left discretion with Grogan. However, in reaching this conclusion, Lord Hatherley articulated the fraud principle with clarity and precision. The jurisdiction to enforce a secret trust rests on the prevention of fraud and the application of the court's equitable jurisdiction over the conscience of the trustee. The court does not act on the will: the doctrine operates entirely outside the Wills Act on the basis of what the trustee accepted. This is why secret trusts are described as operating 'dehors the will'. Lord Hatherley also emphasised the high evidential standard: because secret trusts circumvent the protective requirements of the Wills Act, the court requires the clearest possible evidence before enforcing them.

legal_principle: The court held that a fully secret trust failed where the testator communicated the existence of the trust to the legatee before death but did not communicate the identity of the beneficiary or the terms of the trust until after the will was executed. For a fully secret trust to be valid, both the existence of the trust and its terms must be communicated to and accepted by the trustee before or at the time of the will. Where the terms are only communicated after the will, the trustee holds on resulting trust for the estate.
key_quote: For a fully secret trust, both the existence and terms of the trust must be communicated to the trustee before or at the time the will is made; communication of terms after the will leaves the trustee holding on resulting trust.
full_summary: Re Boyes is an important Chancery Division decision that addresses the precise requirements for effective communication in fully secret trust cases. The testator left all his property to his solicitor, George Boyes, apparently absolutely. He told Boyes during his lifetime that he wished him to hold the property for certain unnamed purposes, but did not communicate the terms or objects of the trust until after the will had been made. Documents discovered after death indicated the intended beneficiaries. Kay J held that the trust failed. While an earlier communication of the existence of the trust obligation was made, the terms of that obligation — the identity of the beneficiaries — were only communicated after the execution of the will. For a fully secret trust, the communication must extend to both the existence and the terms of the trust, and both must be communicated before or at the time of the will. Because the terms were communicated too late, the secret trust could not be enforced as an express trust. However, Boyes could not keep the property for himself either, as he had given an undertaking that he would not take beneficially. Accordingly, the property was held on resulting trust for the testator's estate. Re Boyes is an important reminder that partial communication — communicating the existence but not the terms of a trust — is insufficient.

legal_principle: Brightman J held that where the testator left his bungalow to his housekeeper on the strength of her oral promise to leave it in turn to his son on her death, this created a valid fully secret trust. The case establishes that a secret trust can operate over land and that the obligation may be of a 'floating' nature — attaching to any property the secret trustee happens to own at the time of their death, unless dissipated during their lifetime.
key_quote: A fully secret trust may validly operate over real property; an oral promise to leave property to a third party after the trustee's death creates an enforceable trust obligation.
full_summary: Ottaway v Norman is a notable Chancery Division decision that confirms the ability of a fully secret trust to operate over real property and introduces the concept of the floating secret trust. Harry Ottaway left his bungalow to his housekeeper Miss Hodges by his will, having obtained from her an oral promise that she would leave the bungalow and its contents, together with any money remaining at her death, to his son William. Miss Hodges made a will in favour of William but subsequently changed it in favour of the defendants. William claimed that she held the property on a fully secret trust. Brightman J held in William's favour. He accepted that the essentials of a fully secret trust — a clear intention on the part of the testator to create a trust, communication of that intention to the legatee, and acceptance by the legatee — were all established on the evidence. He further held that there was no rule of law preventing a fully secret trust from operating over real property, notwithstanding the requirements of section 53 of the Law of Property Act 1925 (or its equivalent), because the trust arises by operation of equity to prevent fraud. The more difficult aspect of the case concerns the floating element: Brightman J accepted that the obligation could extend to any property the trustee had at death, provided it had not been dissipated during her lifetime.

legal_principle: The court addressed the situation where a secret trust obligation is communicated to one of two joint tenant legatees. Where communication is made to one joint tenant before the execution of the will, both joint tenants are bound.
