If communication is only made after the will, the joint tenant who received communication is bound but the other takes free of the trust. The case deals with the interplay between secret trusts and the attestation problem in co-legatee situations.
key_quote: Where a secret trust is communicated to one of two joint tenants before the will is executed, both are bound by it; communication to only one after the will leaves the other free of the obligation.
full_summary: Re Stead addresses one of the more technical aspects of secret trust law: the position where a trust obligation is communicated to only one of several legatees. Farwell J analysed the situation by distinguishing between joint tenants and tenants in common, and between communication before and after the execution of the will. Where the legatees take as joint tenants and communication is made to one before the will is executed, the fraud of the one binds the other: if the legatee who accepted the obligation were permitted to take beneficially, the co-legatee would also benefit, as joint tenants take together. Equity therefore binds both. Where communication is only made to one joint tenant after the will has been executed, the position is different: the will has already been drawn up by reference to both legatees, and only the one who accepted the obligation is bound. The other is not tainted by the fraud. Where legatees take as tenants in common, each takes a distinct share, and only the one who received communication and accepted the obligation is bound, regardless of when communication occurred. Re Stead remains the leading authority on this point and is an important part of any examination on secret trusts.

legal_principle: The court held that a secret trust obligation can be established by the trustee's acquiescence or nod of acceptance in response to the testator's communication of the trust terms, without express verbal acceptance. The secret trustee's silence or nod in circumstances where a reasonable person would have objected if unwilling to accept creates an implied acceptance sufficient to bind the trustee.
key_quote: Acquiescence or silent acceptance in circumstances where objection would be expected suffices to establish communication and acceptance of a secret trust.
full_summary: Moss v Cooper is an early authority that addresses the mode of acceptance required to establish a fully secret trust. The testator communicated the existence of the trust to the legatees and their response was, effectively, acquiescence: they did not expressly accept the obligation in words but nodded or remained silent in circumstances where, had they been unwilling to accept, they would have been expected to protest. The Vice-Chancellor held that this was sufficient acceptance. The principle established in Moss v Cooper is that acceptance of a secret trust need not be express: it may be inferred from the conduct of the legatee in the circumstances of communication. Where a testator informs a person that property is to be left to them on trust and they respond by nod, gesture, or silence in circumstances where dissent would naturally have been expressed, equity treats this as sufficient acceptance to bind the conscience of the legatee. This principle reflects the fraud basis of secret trust enforcement: the fraud lies in accepting the property on the strength of an undertaking (however informally expressed) and then reneging on it. Moss v Cooper is cited in standard equity textbooks as authority for the proposition that acceptance of a secret trust may be implied.

court: England and Wales Court of Appeal
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal held that where a testator bequests a sum on trust (half-secret trust) and later adds an additional sum in a codicil to be held on the 'same trusts', the additional sum is not bound by the original secret trust communication unless there has been fresh communication to the trustee of the intention to add it. A trustee who has not accepted the obligation in relation to the additional property cannot be bound by the earlier communication alone.
key_quote: A fresh communication to the trustee is required before additional property bequeathed in a codicil can be held on the same secret trust as the original bequest.
full_summary: Re Colin Cooper addresses an important practical problem in secret trust law: what happens when a testator seeks, by codicil, to add further property to an existing secret trust arrangement without making fresh communication to the trustee. The testator had originally bequeathed 5,000 pounds to two trustees to be held on a half-secret trust, and had communicated the terms of the trust to one of the trustees (the other being uncontactable). A later codicil increased the bequest to 10,000 pounds to be held on the 'same trusts'. The Court of Appeal held that the additional 5,000 pounds was not held on the secret trust. The reason was that at the time of the codicil, no fresh communication had been made to either trustee about the additional sum. The trustee who had accepted the original obligation had only accepted it in relation to the original 5,000 pounds. The additional amount required its own fresh communication and acceptance to be validly comprised in the secret trust. As this had not occurred, the additional sum was held on resulting trust for the testator's estate. Re Colin Cooper is cited as authority for the rule that each increment of property added to a secret trust arrangement requires its own communication and acceptance.

legal_principle: The Irish High Court applied the principles of secret trusts law in an Irish testamentary context, confirming that the fraud-based equitable doctrine applies in Irish law and that a fully secret trust must be established by clear and satisfactory evidence of communication, acceptance, and reliance by the testator on the trustee's promise. The case affirms the continuing vitality of secret trust doctrine in Irish equity.
key_quote: The fraud-based doctrine of secret trusts applies in Irish law; clear evidence of communication and acceptance is required before equity will impose a trust on a legatee.
full_summary: Re Watters' Will Trusts is an Irish High Court decision that confirms the applicability of secret trust doctrine in Irish law. The case involved a dispute over the estate of a deceased testator who had allegedly communicated a secret trust obligation to a legatee during his lifetime. The legatee denied the obligation, and the claimants sought to establish a fully secret trust by parol evidence. The court considered the requirements for a valid fully secret trust and affirmed that Irish equity adopts the same fraud-based approach as English equity. The jurisdiction to enforce a secret trust rests on the prevention of unconscionable conduct: where a testator makes a testamentary disposition in reliance on the acceptance by the legatee of an obligation to hold the property for a third party, equity will enforce that obligation to prevent the legatee from taking free of it. The court emphasised the high standard of evidence required, consistent with the approach of Lord Hatherley in McCormick v Grogan. The evidence in this case did not satisfy the court that all the necessary elements of a fully secret trust had been established, and the claim accordingly failed. Re Watters' Will Trusts is a useful illustration of the application of secret trust principles in Irish courts and confirms that Irish equity closely tracks English equity on this topic.

legal_principle: The Irish High Court considered the requirements for a valid half-secret trust in Irish law. The court confirmed that for a half-secret trust to be enforceable, the trust terms must have been communicated to the trustee before or at the time of execution of the will and that the same principles applicable in English law govern Irish secret trusts. The case illustrates the application of equity principles to Irish testamentary dispositions.
key_quote: An Irish half-secret trust requires communication and acceptance of its terms before or at the time of the will, in accordance with the principles established in Blackwell v Blackwell.
full_summary: Re Prendiville is a significant Irish High Court decision on the requirements for a valid half-secret trust in Irish law. The testator had made a will directing his trustees to hold certain property on trust for purposes that the court was asked to determine from extrinsic evidence. The critical question was whether the terms of the alleged trust had been communicated to the trustees before or at the time of the execution of the will, as required by the rule in Blackwell v Blackwell [1929] AC 318. The court undertook a detailed review of the evidence of communication and accepted that the Blackwell v Blackwell rule applies in Irish law without modification. Accordingly, for a half-secret trust to be enforced in Ireland, it is necessary to establish: first, that the will shows on its face that the trustees are to hold on trust (thus identifying the arrangement as a half-secret trust); second, that the terms of the trust were communicated to the trustees prior to or contemporaneously with the execution of the will; and third, that the trustees accepted the obligation. The case is notable for confirming the full reception of English secret trust doctrine into Irish law and for its analysis of evidence of communication in a specifically Irish context. It is frequently cited in Irish equity and succession textbooks.

court: England and Wales Court of Appeal
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal (Megarry J's analysis) classified resulting trusts into two categories: (1) automatic resulting trusts, which arise where an express trust fails wholly or in part, leaving the beneficial interest undisposed of; and (2) presumed resulting trusts, which arise from a voluntary conveyance or purchase in the name of another, rebutted by evidence of a gift or advancement. The case also confirmed that once a resulting trust had been extinguished by a valid new declaration, the property no longer resulted back to the transferor.
key_quote: Resulting trusts arise in two main situations: where an express trust fails leaving the beneficial interest undisposed of (automatic), and where property is transferred voluntarily or in the name of another (presumed).
full_summary: Re Vandervell's Trusts (No 2) [1974] Ch 269 is a foundational English authority on the classification and operation of resulting trusts. The dispute arose from the complex arrangements surrounding Vandervell's option over shares that had been the subject of earlier litigation. Megarry J's landmark analysis in the Court of Appeal divided resulting trusts into two distinct categories. First, automatic resulting trusts arise by operation of law whenever an express trust fails to exhaust the entire beneficial interest — the undisposed beneficial interest automatically results back to the settlor. Second, presumed resulting trusts arise in two classic situations: a voluntary conveyance of property into the name of another person, and a purchase of property in the name of another. In both instances, a presumption of resulting trust arises in favour of the transferor or the person who provided the purchase money, which may be rebutted by evidence of an intention to make a gift or, in appropriate relationships, by the presumption of advancement. The Court of Appeal also confirmed that a valid new declaration of trust, made by a person entitled to make it, can extinguish a pre-existing resulting trust and vest the beneficial interest in a new beneficiary. The case remains essential reading for any understanding of resulting trust taxonomy and is regularly cited in academic and judicial analyses of how equity allocates undisposed beneficial interests. It is closely connected with the broader Vandervell litigation, which raised issues of perpetuities, options, and the formalities required to extinguish equitable interests.

case_name: Westdeutsche Landesbank Girozentrale v Islington LBC
legal_principle: Lord Browne-Wilkinson stated that a resulting trust arises only where the transferor did not intend the beneficial interest to pass, and that a trust (including a constructive trust) only arises when the conscience of the recipient is affected. The case also confirmed that the plaintiff could not establish a proprietary remedy over a void interest rate swap payment because there was no retained beneficial interest from the outset. The 'conscience' requirement is now central to constructive trust analysis.
key_quote: A resulting trust arises only where the transferor did not intend the beneficial interest to pass; a constructive trust arises where the recipient's conscience is affected by knowledge of the circumstances.
full_summary: Westdeutsche Landesbank Girozentrale v Islington LBC [1996] AC 669 is a seminal House of Lords decision arising from an ultra vires interest rate swap entered into by Islington London Borough Council. The bank sought to recover compound interest on sums paid under the void contract, arguing that the council held those sums on resulting or constructive trust. The House of Lords rejected the proprietary claim. Lord Browne-Wilkinson delivered the leading speech and set out a theoretical account of how trusts arise. He held that a resulting trust arises only where the transferor has not intended the beneficial interest to pass — if the transferor intended an outright transfer (even if later shown to be on a void contract), no resulting trust arises from the outset. A constructive trust, meanwhile, can only arise once the conscience of the legal owner is affected by knowledge of the relevant facts. At the time of receipt the council was unaware the contract was void, so its conscience was not affected and no constructive trust arose. The decision significantly curtailed the use of resulting and constructive trusts as restitutionary devices in commercial law. Academically, Lord Browne-Wilkinson's analysis of resulting trusts — in particular his rejection of the 'no intention' basis in favour of a positive intention requirement — has been both influential and controversial. The case is frequently cited alongside Re Vandervell's Trusts (No 2) in discussions of resulting trust theory.

legal_principle: Megarry J held that a mail-order company that had placed customer advance payments into a separate bank account held those funds on trust for the customers. The creation of a separate account with the clear intention that the funds would be used only for specific purposes established a trust, and customers' money was therefore not available to the company's general creditors on insolvency. The case is an important authority on the use of express trusts to protect customers in commercial insolvency.
key_quote: The opening of a separate bank account in the name of a trust, with the intention that funds held therein belong to customers, creates a valid trust binding in insolvency.
full_summary: Re Kayford Ltd [1975] 1 WLR 279 is an important Chancery Division decision on the use of trust structures to protect customer deposits in commercial insolvency. Kayford Ltd, a mail-order company, had received advance payments from customers for goods not yet delivered. Anticipating financial difficulty, the company — acting on legal advice — opened a separate bank account called the 'Customers' Trust Deposit Account' and paid customer advance payments into it. When the company entered insolvent liquidation, the question arose whether those funds were part of the general assets available to creditors or whether they were held on trust for the customers. Megarry J held that a valid trust had been created. The company had manifested a clear intention to hold the funds separately for the customers, the subject matter was identifiable (the funds in the separate account), and the beneficiaries (the customers) were ascertainable. The fact that the trust was created by the company itself — rather than being imposed by the court — did not prevent it from being valid. Customers were therefore entitled to recover their advance payments in priority to the general body of creditors. Re Kayford is frequently paired with Barclays Bank v Quistclose Investments Ltd in the study of commercial trusts and the use of trust structures to achieve priority on insolvency. It raises important policy questions about whether trust law should be available to sophisticated businesses as a device to confer priority on certain creditors.

case_name: Barclays Bank Ltd v Quistclose Investments Ltd
legal_principle: The House of Lords held that where money is lent for a specific purpose and paid into a separate account to be used for that purpose only, the money is held on trust for the lender if the purpose fails. The primary trust is for the specific purpose; if that fails, a secondary resulting trust arises in favour of the lender. This 'Quistclose trust' is enforceable on insolvency and prevents the funds becoming available to general creditors.
key_quote: Where money is lent for a specific purpose and paid into a separate account, it is held on trust for the lender if the purpose fails; the lender is not merely an unsecured creditor.
full_summary: Barclays Bank Ltd v Quistclose Investments Ltd [1970] AC 567 is the foundational House of Lords authority on what has come to be known as the Quistclose trust. Rolls Razor Ltd was in financial difficulty and required funds to pay a declared dividend to shareholders. Quistclose Investments agreed to lend the necessary sum on the express condition that it be used solely for the purpose of paying the dividend. The money was paid into a separate bank account at Barclays Bank. Before the dividend could be paid, Rolls Razor went into liquidation. The question was whether Barclays, which had a right of set-off against Rolls Razor, could treat the sum in the separate account as available to discharge Rolls Razor's overdraft, or whether Quistclose was entitled to recover it. The House of Lords unanimously held for Quistclose. Lord Wilberforce held that the money was impressed with a primary trust to pay the dividend, and — because that purpose had failed — a secondary resulting trust arose in favour of Quistclose as the lender. The dual trust structure prevented the funds from becoming part of Rolls Razor's general assets on insolvency. The Quistclose trust has since become a significant feature of insolvency and commercial law, allowing lenders to retain a proprietary interest in funds lent for a specific purpose. The case raises important doctrinal questions about the nature of the trust (purpose trust or resulting trust), which were later addressed in Twinsectra Ltd v Yardley [2002].

legal_principle: The House of Lords confirmed and analysed the Quistclose trust, holding that money paid to a solicitor on terms that it was only to be used for a specific purpose was held on Quistclose trust. Lord Millett identified the trust as a species of resulting trust. The case also addressed the test for dishonest assistance in breach of trust, preferring a combined objective/subjective approach (the Twinsectra test) over a purely objective standard.
key_quote: A Quistclose trust is a resulting trust arising from the lender's retention of the beneficial interest; the borrower has power to apply the money for the stated purpose but no beneficial interest in it unless and until so applied.
full_summary: Twinsectra Ltd v Yardley [2002] 2 AC 164 is a pivotal House of Lords decision that both confirmed the Quistclose trust doctrine and provided an authoritative analysis of its theoretical basis. Twinsectra lent money to Yardley on terms recorded in an undertaking given by Yardley's solicitor, Sims, that the funds would be used solely for the acquisition of property and for no other purpose. Sims paid the money to another solicitor, Leach, who released it to Yardley for general purposes in breach of the undertaking. Twinsectra sued Leach for dishonest assistance in a breach of trust. The House of Lords held that a Quistclose trust had arisen over the loan moneys. Lord Millett, in a famous analysis, explained that the beneficial interest in Quistclose money never passes to the borrower; the lender retains the beneficial interest subject to the borrower's power to apply the money for the specified purpose. If the purpose fails, the resulting trust in favour of the lender is reactivated. On dishonest assistance, the majority adopted a combined objective/subjective test: the defendant must act in a way which is dishonest by the standards of honest and reasonable people, and must also subjectively appreciate that what he is doing is by those standards dishonest. This Twinsectra test was subsequently reconsidered in Barlow Clowes International Ltd v Eurotrust International Ltd [2006], which moved closer to an objective standard.

legal_principle: The Privy Council held that a surplus in a pension fund that could not be applied cy-pres to the original purposes resulted back to the contributing employers and employees on resulting trust. Lord Millett rejected the argument that a resulting trust could not arise because the transferors lacked a subjective intention to create one, confirming that resulting trusts arise by operation of law and do not require the parties to intend them.
key_quote: A resulting trust arises by operation of law whenever property is transferred and the beneficial interest is not exhausted; it does not depend on any subjective intention of the parties to create a trust.
full_summary: Air Jamaica Ltd v Charlton [1999] 1 WLR 1399 is a Privy Council decision arising from the winding up of a pension scheme. When the scheme was discontinued, a surplus remained after all pension obligations had been satisfied. The pension trust deed contained a clause purporting to exclude any resulting trust in favour of the contributors. The question was whether, notwithstanding that exclusion, the surplus resulted back to the employers and employees who had contributed to the fund. The Privy Council, in a judgment delivered by Lord Millett, held that the surplus was held on resulting trust for the contributing employers and employees in proportion to their respective contributions. Lord Millett rejected the argument that a resulting trust could not arise because the parties had not subjectively intended to create one. A resulting trust is imposed by equity as a matter of law — it is not a form of implied express trust requiring intention. It arises whenever property is transferred and the transferor has not disposed of the entire beneficial interest. The exclusion clause in the deed could not defeat a resulting trust because a resulting trust arises by operation of law, not by agreement. The decision is also significant for confirming the proportionate basis on which resulting trust shares are calculated where multiple parties have contributed to a fund. Air Jamaica is frequently discussed alongside Westdeutsche Landesbank in debates about the theoretical basis of resulting trusts.

legal_principle: Goff J held that a trust to maintain a sports ground for the benefit of identified company employees was valid as a trust for persons (not merely for a purpose), because the employees had a sufficient interest to enforce the trust. The Denley principle operates as an exception to the beneficiary principle: a trust that has an ostensible purpose but which directly and indirectly benefits identifiable persons who can enforce it is valid.
key_quote: A trust which, though expressed as a purpose trust, directly benefits identifiable persons who can enforce it is valid; the beneficiary principle is satisfied.
full_summary: Re Denley's Trust Deed [1969] 1 Ch 373 is a significant Chancery Division decision on the borderline between valid purpose trusts and invalid non-charitable purpose trusts. A company transferred land to trustees to be maintained and used as a sports ground primarily for the benefit of the company's employees, with any surplus use available to others at the trustees' discretion. The question was whether the trust was void as a purpose trust that lacked human beneficiaries capable of enforcing it — the rule in Morice v Bishop of Durham. Goff J upheld the trust. He reasoned that although the trust was expressed in terms of a purpose (maintaining a sports ground), it directly and indirectly benefited the employees as identifiable individuals who had a sufficient interest to invoke the court's jurisdiction to enforce the trust. This distinguished the case from pure abstract purpose trusts such as Re Astor's Settlement Trusts. The Denley principle is important because it provides a practical route by which apparently purpose-oriented trusts can be saved: if identifiable persons benefit sufficiently from the performance of the purpose to be granted locus standi to enforce it, the trust is valid. The principle has been applied and discussed in subsequent cases on non-charitable purpose trusts and is a staple of equity examination questions dealing with the beneficiary principle and its qualifications.

case_name: Re Money Markets International Stockbrokers Ltd
legal_principle: The Irish High Court applied Quistclose trust principles to client funds held by a stockbroker. The court held that client money segregated in a client account and received for a specific investment purpose was held on Quistclose-style trust, not available to general creditors on insolvency. The case extended English Quistclose principles into Irish commercial trust law.
key_quote: Client money received by a stockbroker for specific investment purposes and segregated in a client account is held on trust for clients and is not available to general creditors on insolvency.
full_summary: Re Money Markets International Stockbrokers Ltd [2003] 3 IR 437 is an important Irish High Court decision that applied and extended the Quistclose trust doctrine to the regulation and insolvency of stockbrokers in Ireland. The case arose when Money Markets International Stockbrokers Ltd, a regulated stockbroking firm, went into receivership. Client funds had been received by the firm for the specific purpose of making investments on clients' behalf and had been segregated in a client account as required by financial services regulation. The question was whether these client funds were available to the firm's general creditors or were held on trust for the clients. The Irish High Court held that the client funds were held on trust. The segregation of the funds in a dedicated client account, combined with the specific purpose for which the money had been received (to invest on behalf of clients), established the conditions necessary for the recognition of a Quistclose-style trust. The firm had no beneficial interest in the client money and could not apply it for any purpose other than investment on the clients' behalf. The court expressly drew on the English Quistclose line of authority — including Barclays Bank v Quistclose Investments Ltd and Twinsectra v Yardley — and applied those principles in the Irish context. The decision is a key Irish authority for the proposition that Quistclose trust reasoning is part of Irish equity and that regulatory segregation requirements in the financial services sector are consistent with and reinforce the imposition of a trust.

legal_principle: The Supreme Court of Ireland considered the presumption of resulting trust arising from a voluntary transfer of property. The court confirmed that where property is voluntarily transferred into the name of another without any consideration passing, a presumption of resulting trust arises in favour of the transferor unless rebutted by evidence of an intention to make a gift. The case applies classical resulting trust principles to an Irish property dispute.
key_quote: A voluntary transfer without consideration raises a presumption of resulting trust; the transferee must adduce evidence of a gift or advancement to rebut this presumption.
full_summary: Re Hughes [1970] IR 198 is a Supreme Court of Ireland decision addressing the presumption of resulting trust in the context of a voluntary transfer of property. The case concerned a transfer of property made without valuable consideration passing from the transferee. On the death of the transferor, a dispute arose as to whether the transferee held the property beneficially or on resulting trust for the transferor's estate. The Supreme Court held that the voluntary transfer of property without consideration raised a presumption of resulting trust in favour of the transferor. Where nothing is paid by the transferee in exchange for the transfer, equity presumes that the transferor did not intend to make an outright gift and that the beneficial interest was intended to remain with the transferor. The transferee accordingly holds the legal title on resulting trust unless they can adduce sufficient evidence to rebut the presumption — for example, by demonstrating a clear intention to make a gift or establishing a relationship in which the presumption of advancement applies (such as a parent-child or husband-wife relationship at that time). The court applied classical English resulting trust principles and confirmed their reception and application in Irish equity. Re Hughes is cited as authority for the survival of the presumption of resulting trust in Irish law and for the conditions under which the presumption of advancement may rebut it. The decision is an important reference point for the comparative study of resulting trusts in England and Ireland.

case_name: Mac-Jordan Construction Ltd v Brookmount Erostin Ltd
court: England and Wales Court of Appeal
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal held that a contractual right to a retention fund does not give rise to a proprietary trust interest in the absence of a specifically identifiable fund. The mere obligation to pay over money does not create a trust; for a Quistclose or commercial trust to arise, there must be a specifically identified and segregated fund. The absence of segregation is fatal to any proprietary claim on insolvency.
key_quote: A contractual obligation to pay money does not create a trust; for a proprietary interest to arise in a fund, the fund must be specifically identified and segregated.
full_summary: Mac-Jordan Construction Ltd v Brookmount Erostin Ltd [1992] BCLC 350 is a Court of Appeal decision that imposes a significant practical limit on the use of trust arguments to achieve priority on insolvency in the construction industry context. Mac-Jordan, a building contractor, was owed a retention sum under a building contract with Brookmount. A term of the contract required Brookmount to set up a separate retention fund into which the withheld sums would be paid. Brookmount failed to establish the separate fund and went into administrative receivership. Mac-Jordan claimed a proprietary interest over the retention sum on the basis that it was held on trust. The Court of Appeal rejected the claim. Scott LJ held that a contractual obligation to establish a fund and hold it on trust does not, by itself, create a trust. A trust can only attach to an identified and existing fund; a mere in personam obligation to create such a fund in the future does not give rise to proprietary rights. Because Brookmount had never actually segregated the retention money, there was no identifiable trust fund to which Mac-Jordan's proprietary claim could attach. The decision applies and reinforces the general principle — also central to Quistclose trust analysis — that the certainty of subject matter required for a valid trust (the identifiability of the trust fund) must actually be satisfied in practice, not merely contemplated by contract. The case is an important reminder that contractual drafting alone cannot substitute for actual segregation when trust protection on insolvency is sought.

legal_principle: Lord King LC held that a trustee who renews a lease for his own benefit — even where the renewal was refused for the trust — holds the renewed lease on constructive trust for the beneficiary. The strict rule prevents trustees from profiting from their fiduciary position, even where the trust could not itself have benefited. The rule applies regardless of fraud or bad faith; the mere fact of profit from a fiduciary position suffices.
key_quote: A trustee who renews a lease for his own benefit where the renewal was refused for the trust holds it on constructive trust for the beneficiary; no equitable justification can override this principle.
full_summary: Keech v Sandford (1726) Sel Cas Ch 61 is one of the oldest and most frequently cited authorities in the law of fiduciary obligations. A trustee held a lease of a market on trust for an infant beneficiary. When the lease came up for renewal, the trustee sought to renew it on behalf of the trust, but the lessor refused because the infant could not be bound by covenants. The trustee then renewed the lease for his own personal benefit. Lord King LC held that the trustee must hold the renewed lease on constructive trust for the beneficiary. The decision is remarkable for its strict and uncompromising nature: the trustee was not guilty of any fraud and the trust itself could not have obtained the renewal. Nevertheless, equity intervened because the trustee had profited from his fiduciary position. Lord King reasoned that to allow a trustee to profit in circumstances where the trust could not itself benefit would open the door to abuse and would encourage trustees to engineer situations where the trust's interests could be defeated for personal gain. The rule in Keech v Sandford is a cornerstone of the no-profit rule that governs all fiduciaries. It operates with stringent strictness and without any requirement of dishonesty or bad faith on the part of the fiduciary. The principle has been applied to company directors, solicitors, agents, and all other categories of fiduciary who seek to profit from opportunities that come to them by reason of their fiduciary position. It is closely connected to the no-conflict rule articulated in Bray v Ford.

legal_principle: The House of Lords held that a solicitor to a trust who used information acquired in a fiduciary capacity to make a profit on the purchase of shares (in which the trust also acquired shares) was a constructive trustee of the profit, even though he had acted in good faith and the trust itself had benefited. A fiduciary who profits from their position without fully informed consent of all beneficiaries must account for the profit on a constructive trust basis.
key_quote: A fiduciary who profits from the use of information acquired in that capacity holds the profit on constructive trust, even if they acted honestly and the trust itself benefited.
full_summary: Boardman v Phipps [1967] 2 AC 46 is one of the most debated House of Lords decisions in the law of fiduciary obligations. Tom Boardman, a solicitor acting for the Phipps family trust, identified an opportunity to acquire shares in a company in which the trust held a minority stake. Acting with one of the adult beneficiaries, Boardman purchased shares in the company in his own name. The investment was highly profitable and the trust also benefited. Boardman and his co-purchaser acted throughout in complete good faith, kept the trustees informed, and believed they had consent. However, the House of Lords (by a bare majority of three to two) held that Boardman was a constructive trustee of the profit he made. The majority held that Boardman had used information and an opportunity that came to him only because of his fiduciary position, and that this was sufficient to engage the no-profit rule without any requirement of dishonesty. The decision was not unanimous — Lords Upjohn and Dilhorne dissented on the ground that the information used was not truly confidential to the trust. In recognition of his good faith, Boardman was awarded a liberal allowance for the value of his services. Boardman v Phipps remains controversial: it represents the strictest possible application of the no-profit rule and has been criticised for imposing onerous liability on fiduciaries who act honestly and beneficially. It is a central case in all equity syllabi dealing with fiduciary duties.

legal_principle: The court held that a trustee who used their position as trustee to appoint themselves as a company director and then received directors' fees from that company held those fees on constructive trust for the beneficiaries. The no-profit rule applies not only to direct profits from trust property but also to remuneration received as a result of exercising powers conferred by the trust.
key_quote: A trustee who uses their position to appoint themselves as director and receive fees holds those fees on constructive trust for the beneficiaries; the no-profit rule extends to all remuneration obtained through exercise of trust powers.
full_summary: Re Macadam [1946] Ch 73 is a Chancery Division decision that extends the no-profit rule to cover remuneration received by a trustee as a result of exercising a power vested in them by the trust instrument. The trustees of a trust held shares in a company and the trust instrument gave them the power to appoint directors to the company. The trustees used this power to appoint themselves as directors and subsequently received directors' fees from the company. The question was whether the trustees were obliged to account to the trust for those fees. Cohen J held that they were. The trustees had obtained the opportunity to receive directors' fees by virtue of the exercise of a power conferred upon them in their capacity as trustees. The no-profit rule is not confined to profits derived directly from trust property; it extends to any benefit obtained through the exercise of a trust power or through the use of a position of trust. Because the trustees would not have been appointed as directors but for their position as trustees, the fees were profits derived from the trust relationship and had to be held on constructive trust for the beneficiaries. Re Macadam is frequently discussed alongside the trustee remuneration rules and the Cradock v Piper exception, and it illustrates the breadth of the no-profit rule in its application to indirect and incidental benefits flowing from fiduciary office.

legal_principle: Lord Herschell stated the fundamental equitable rule that a person in a fiduciary position is not, unless otherwise expressly provided, entitled to make a profit out of their trust or to place themselves in a position where their interest conflicts with their duty. This is not a matter of morality but of a strict rule that prevents the possibility of conflicts between fiduciary duty and personal interest.
key_quote: It is an inflexible rule of equity that a person in a fiduciary position is not entitled to make a profit out of that trust, and is not allowed to place himself in a position where his interest and his duty conflict.
full_summary: Bray v Ford [1896] AC 44 is the foundational House of Lords authority for the articulation of the no-profit and no-conflict rules that govern all fiduciaries. The case itself concerned the question of whether a trustee of a charitable body was entitled to payment for work done as a solicitor on behalf of the charity. The House of Lords held that the trustee was not entitled to such payment, absent express authorisation. Lord Herschell's dictum — now one of the most quoted passages in equity — states that it is an inflexible rule of equity that a person in a fiduciary position is not allowed to make a profit out of that trust, and is not permitted to place himself in a position where his interest and duty conflict. Critically, Lord Herschell emphasised that this rule is not based on morality or any finding that the fiduciary has behaved dishonestly. It is a prophylactic rule designed to prevent the possibility of conflict arising between a fiduciary's personal interests and the duty they owe to their principal. The strictness of the rule means it operates even where the fiduciary has acted with complete honesty and in perfect good faith. Bray v Ford is the conceptual anchor for the entire body of fiduciary law dealing with the no-profit and no-conflict rules. It is cited in virtually every significant case on fiduciary duties, from Keech v Sandford through to Boardman v Phipps and beyond, and is a starting point for any examination answer on fiduciary obligations.

legal_principle: The court established that a trustee who is also a solicitor may, as an exception to the no-profit rule, charge their usual professional costs when acting as solicitor in litigation on behalf of the trust, provided they also appear for non-trust parties in the same litigation. This exception — the Cradock v Piper rule — is a narrow qualification to the general prohibition on trustee remuneration.
key_quote: A trustee-solicitor may charge professional costs for acting in litigation where other parties are also represented and the same costs would have been incurred regardless of the trustee's position.
full_summary: Cradock v Piper (1850) 1 Mac & G 664 is a Court of Chancery decision that established a limited and specific exception to the general no-profit rule as it applies to trustee-solicitors. The general rule — articulated in Bray v Ford — is that a trustee may not profit from their trust position. Where a trustee is also a solicitor, this means they cannot charge professional fees for work done in connection with the trust without express authorisation. However, Cradock v Piper recognised a narrow exception: where a trustee-solicitor is retained in litigation and acts not only for the trust but also for non-trust parties in the same proceedings, they may charge their usual professional costs, provided the costs are not increased by reason of their acting for the trust as well as the other parties. The rationale is that the same costs would have been incurred regardless of the trustee's involvement, so the trust does not bear any additional cost attributable to the no-profit rule. The Cradock v Piper exception has been described as anomalous and is strictly construed. It does not permit a trustee-solicitor to charge for work done exclusively for the trust, and it operates only where litigation is involved and additional parties are being represented simultaneously. The exception has been superseded to a considerable extent by the Trustee Act 2000 provisions on trustee remuneration but retains historical and doctrinal significance in equity syllabi.

court: England and Wales Court of Appeal
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal held that the self-dealing rule did not apply to prevent an executor who had renounced his executorship from purchasing estate property at auction where he had not exercised any significant fiduciary function and the sale was at full market value. The case establishes that the self-dealing rule may be displaced where the fiduciary has had no real opportunity to influence the transaction and the sale was at a fair price.
key_quote: The self-dealing rule does not apply to prevent an executor who has renounced probate from bidding at an auction of estate property where the sale was conducted at full value and the executor had no real influence over it.
full_summary: Holder v Holder [1968] Ch 353 is a Court of Appeal decision that introduces an important qualification to the self-dealing rule in the law of fiduciary obligations. The self-dealing rule provides that a trustee or other fiduciary who purchases trust property from themselves in their fiduciary capacity is liable to have the transaction set aside at the option of the beneficiaries, regardless of the fairness of the price paid. The rule is underpinned by the conflict of interest between the fiduciary's duty to obtain the best price for the beneficiaries and their personal interest as a purchaser in paying the lowest possible price. In Holder v Holder, an executor who had intermeddled in the estate but subsequently renounced his executorship purchased estate property at a public auction. The Court of Appeal held that the self-dealing rule did not apply to vitiate the purchase. The executor had taken no significant steps in the administration of the estate that would have given him any advantage over other bidders, had renounced before the auction, and the property had been sold by public auction at full market value. Danckwerts and Harman LJJ also applied considerations of fair dealing. The decision draws the important distinction between the strict self-dealing rule (where the fiduciary exercises their fiduciary powers in the transaction) and the fair dealing rule (where the fiduciary simply purchases from a beneficiary). Holder v Holder is a nuanced case that demonstrates equity's capacity to temper strict rules where their rationale is absent.

legal_principle: Megarry V-C made important observations on the enforcement of positive obligations in equity and distinguished between personal obligations to perform and obligations that can be enforced as proprietary claims. The case is frequently cited for the distinction between a 'trust of a promise' (not recognised) and an express proprietary trust, and for its analysis of when equity will enforce a positive obligation as opposed to a personal one.
key_quote: Equity does not enforce a trust of a promise to pay money as a proprietary trust; there must be an identifiable trust fund over which a proprietary claim can be asserted.
full_summary: Tito v Waddell (No 2) [1977] Ch 106 is a lengthy and wide-ranging Chancery Division judgment delivered by Megarry V-C arising from the phosphate mining of Ocean Island (Banaba) and the relocation of the Banaban people. The substantive claims involved alleged breaches of obligation by the Crown and the British Phosphate Commission. Among the many equitable issues considered, the case is particularly noted for Megarry V-C's analysis of the distinction between obligations enforceable as proprietary trust claims and purely personal obligations. He held that equity does not recognise a 'trust of a promise' — that is, a promise to pay money or perform some act cannot itself constitute the subject matter of a trust. For a trust to arise, there must be an identifiable and existing fund or property over which the trust can attach. A mere contractual or personal obligation, however binding in personam, does not create a proprietary equitable interest enforceable against third parties or in insolvency. The case also contains influential observations on the nature of the 'benefit and burden' principle in property law and on the distinction between negative and positive obligations in equity. While the broader claims in the case were largely unsuccessful, the judgment's analysis of trust law and equitable obligations has had lasting significance and is regularly cited in academic literature on proprietary claims, equitable remedies, and the limits of constructive trusts.

court: England and Wales Court of Appeal
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal held that a fiduciary who received a bribe was not a constructive trustee of it — the relationship between the bribe-taker and their principal was one of debtor and creditor, not trustee and beneficiary. This case was overruled by the Privy Council in AG for Hong Kong v Reid [1994] for English and Commonwealth purposes, and the Lister v Stubbs approach is no longer good law for bribes received by fiduciaries.
key_quote: A person who receives a bribe is not a trustee of it for their principal; they are a debtor — overruled by AG for Hong Kong v Reid.
full_summary: Lister v Stubbs (1890) 45 Ch D 1 is an important Court of Appeal decision that, for nearly a century, stood as authority for the proposition that a fiduciary who receives a bribe from a third party does not hold it on constructive trust for their principal but is merely a debtor to the principal for its equivalent value. Stubbs was an employee of Lister who received secret commissions (bribes) from suppliers in exchange for placing orders with them on Lister's behalf. Lister sought an injunction to prevent Stubbs from dealing with investments he had made with the bribe money. The Court of Appeal refused the injunction, holding that the relationship between Lister and Stubbs in respect of the bribe was that of creditor and debtor, not beneficiary and trustee. Stubbs owed Lister the monetary value of the bribes but had not become a trustee of the actual sums or their traceable proceeds. The consequence of this analysis was that Lister had no proprietary claim over Stubbs's investments: if Stubbs had invested the bribes profitably, Lister could not claim the enhanced value. Lister v Stubbs was overruled by the Privy Council in Attorney General for Hong Kong v Reid [1994] 1 AC 324, which held that a fiduciary who receives a bribe holds it on constructive trust from the moment of receipt. The overruling of Lister v Stubbs is confirmed in England and Wales following FHR European Ventures LLP v Cedar Capital Partners LLC [2014] UKSC 45.

case_name: Attorney General for Hong Kong v Reid
legal_principle: The Privy Council overruled Lister v Stubbs and held that a fiduciary who receives a bribe holds it on constructive trust for their principal immediately on receipt. Where the fiduciary invests the bribe in property that increases in value, the principal is entitled to the increased value as the constructive trust attaches to the bribe and all traceable proceeds. Equity regards as done what ought to be done.
key_quote: A fiduciary who receives a bribe holds it on constructive trust for their principal from the moment of receipt; equity treats as done what ought to be done.
full_summary: Attorney General for Hong Kong v Reid [1994] 1 AC 324 is a landmark Privy Council decision that fundamentally reformed the law on bribes received by fiduciaries. Reid was a senior public prosecutor in Hong Kong who had accepted substantial bribes in exchange for obstructing prosecutions. He used the bribe money to purchase properties in New Zealand that had increased in value. The Privy Council, in a judgment delivered by Lord Templeman, held that Reid held the bribe money and all property purchased with it on constructive trust for the Crown from the moment of receipt. Lord Templeman reasoned that a fiduciary who accepts a bribe commits a breach of fiduciary duty. Equity, treating as done what ought to be done, regards the fiduciary as obliged immediately to pay the bribe to their principal. Since equity treats the obligation as having been performed, the fiduciary is deemed to hold the bribe on constructive trust for the principal. Where the bribe is invested in property that increases in value, the constructive trust attaches to the property and the principal is entitled to the increase in value: the fiduciary cannot profit from their own wrong. The Privy Council expressly overruled Lister v Stubbs. The decision was subsequently endorsed in England by the Supreme Court in FHR European Ventures LLP v Cedar Capital Partners LLC [2014] UKSC 45, which confirmed that secret commissions and bribes are held on constructive trust, giving the principal a proprietary rather than merely personal remedy.

legal_principle: The House of Lords held that company directors who used their fiduciary position to acquire shares in a subsidiary (which the company itself could not afford) and then made a profit on their sale were liable to account to the company for that profit, even though they had acted in good faith and the company had in fact benefited. The no-profit rule operates irrespective of honesty once a profit is made from a fiduciary position.
key_quote: Directors who profit from an opportunity that arose by reason of their fiduciary position must account to the company for that profit, regardless of whether they acted honestly or in good faith.
full_summary: Regal (Hastings) Ltd v Gulliver [1967] 2 AC 134 (decided in 1942 but reported in the Appeal Cases in 1967) is a foundational House of Lords decision on the no-profit rule as applied to company directors. Regal (Hastings) Ltd wished to acquire two cinemas through a wholly owned subsidiary. The lessor of the cinemas required either a personal guarantee from the directors or that the subsidiary have a paid-up share capital of at least £5,000. Regal could only provide £2,000 of the required capital, so the directors subscribed for the remaining shares in their own names. The entire group was subsequently sold at a profit, and the directors made a substantial gain on their shares in the subsidiary. The new controllers of Regal brought an action to recover that gain. The House of Lords held that the directors were liable to account. They had acquired the shares only because of their position as directors of Regal and the opportunity to do so arose from their fiduciary position. The no-profit rule required them to account for the profit to Regal regardless of good faith, honest intent, or the fact that the company could not itself have acquired the shares. The only way to avoid liability would have been to obtain the fully informed approval of the shareholders. Regal v Gulliver is a key English authority on the corporate opportunity doctrine and is invariably studied alongside Boardman v Phipps and Keech v Sandford in the context of fiduciary duties.

legal_principle: The Supreme Court of Ireland held that a gift of property by an elderly and infirm father to his son was voidable for undue influence. The court applied the established equitable doctrine that where a relationship of influence and trust exists between donor and donee, any gift made by the donor to the donee will be presumed to result from undue influence unless the donee can show that the donor received independent advice and understood the nature and effect of the transaction.
key_quote: Where a relationship of trust and influence exists, equity presumes that a gift from the weaker to the stronger party was the result of undue influence; the donee must rebut this presumption by showing independent advice and true understanding.
full_summary: Carroll v Carroll [1999] 4 IR 241 is a significant Supreme Court of Ireland decision on the equitable doctrine of undue influence in the context of family gift transactions. An elderly and infirm father transferred property to his son. Following the father's death, other members of the family challenged the transfer on the ground that it had been procured by undue influence. The Supreme Court held that the gift was voidable and should be set aside. The court applied the presumptive form of undue influence: where a relationship of trust and confidence exists between a donor and a donee, and a gift is made by the donor to the donee, equity presumes that the gift was the product of undue influence unless the donee can rebut that presumption. To rebut the presumption, the donee must demonstrate that the donor received genuinely independent advice from a competent and disinterested adviser, that the donor fully understood the nature and effect of the transaction, and that the donor made the gift as a truly free and independent act. The Supreme Court found that the elderly father had not received adequate independent advice and that the circumstances of the transfer gave rise to the presumption which the son had failed to rebut. Carroll v Carroll is a key Irish authority on presumed undue influence and is regularly cited in Irish equity courses alongside the English authorities such as Allcard v Skinner and Royal Bank of Scotland v Etridge (No 2) as part of the study of fiduciary duties and equitable intervention in transactions procured through influence.

legal_principle: Lord Watson stated the standard applicable to trustee investment: a trustee must take such care as an ordinary prudent man of business would take in managing similar affairs of his own. The trustee must give proper weight to the need to safeguard trust capital as well as to produce income. This 'prudent man of business' standard was later codified in various Trustee Acts and the Trustee Act 2000.
key_quote: As a general rule, the standard required of a trustee in making investments is that which a prudent man of business would take in managing similar affairs of his own.
full_summary: Learoyd v Whiteley is the foundational House of Lords authority on the standard of care expected of trustees when making investment decisions. Lord Watson articulated the classic 'prudent man of business' standard: a trustee must exercise the same care as an ordinary prudent man of business would exercise when managing his own financial affairs of a similar kind. This means the trustee must seek both to preserve the capital of the trust fund and to produce a reasonable income for the beneficiaries. The standard does not demand perfect investment judgment, but it does require the trustee to avoid speculative or hazardous ventures and to give proper consideration to the safety of the capital. The case arose from a trustee's investment in a brickfield mortgage which later proved inadequate security. The House of Lords found the trustee in breach of duty for investing in an asset carrying too great a risk of capital loss. The prudent man of business standard was enormously influential: it was adopted in subsequent legislation, including the Trustee Investments Act 1961, and formed the basis of the statutory duty of care now found in the Trustee Act 2000 (England) and the Trustee Act 1893 as applied in Ireland. The case remains the starting point for any analysis of trustee investment duties.

legal_principle: Megarry V-C held that trustees must exercise their investment powers in the best financial interests of the beneficiaries and cannot refuse to invest in a class of investments for ethical or political reasons contrary to those interests. Trustees of pension funds, in particular, are not entitled to subordinate the financial returns of beneficiaries to their own social or political views when making investment decisions.
key_quote: Trustees must exercise their powers in the best financial interests of the beneficiaries and cannot refuse to invest in any class of investments solely for ethical or political reasons where this conflicts with the beneficiaries' financial interests.
full_summary: Cowan v Scargill concerned the trustees of a mineworkers' pension fund, five of whom were nominees of the National Union of Mineworkers led by Arthur Scargill. The NUM trustees refused to approve an investment plan unless it excluded overseas investments and investments in competing energy industries. Megarry V-C held that this refusal was a breach of fiduciary duty. The fundamental duty of trustees is to act in the best interests of the present and future beneficiaries, and in the context of a pension fund that means their best financial interests — maximising the return on investments. Trustees are not entitled to let their own social, political, or ethical views override this duty. The Vice-Chancellor acknowledged that trustees may take ethical considerations into account where two investments are equally advantageous, or where the majority of beneficiaries share the ethical concern and no financial detriment results. However, where ethical considerations conflict with financial interests, financial interests must prevail. The case is the leading authority on ethical investment by trustees and remains highly relevant to debates about ESG (environmental, social, and governance) investment mandates for trustees.

case_name: Nestle v National Westminster Bank plc
court: England and Wales Court of Appeal
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal held that while the bank trustee had failed in its duty to review the trust portfolio and to strike the proper balance between income and capital, the plaintiff beneficiary could not prove loss on the balance of probabilities because the investments retained might equally have been retained by a competent trustee. The case is important both for its statement of the investment review duty and for confirming that causation must be proved separately in investment breach cases.
key_quote: A trustee must regularly review the trust portfolio and strike a proper balance between income and capital; failure to do so is a breach of duty, but damages require proof of loss on the balance of probabilities.
full_summary: Nestle v National Westminster Bank concerned a trust fund managed by the defendant bank from 1922 to 1986. The plaintiff, a remainderman, complained that the bank had failed to review and restructure the portfolio and had favoured income over capital, resulting in a substantial real loss in the value of the fund over more than sixty years. The Court of Appeal agreed that the bank had breached its duty by failing to conduct regular reviews and by failing to achieve a proper balance between income and capital. However, the plaintiff's claim failed because she could not demonstrate on the balance of probabilities that a prudent trustee, properly managing the portfolio, would in fact have invested differently in a way that would have produced a better outcome. The mere existence of a breach of the investment review duty does not automatically establish that loss was caused by the breach. The case is critically important for two reasons: it confirms the positive duty of trustees to review portfolios on a regular basis; and it confirms that causation is a separate and essential element of a claim for equitable compensation for breach of investment duty.

case_name: Bartlett v Barclays Bank Trust Co Ltd
legal_principle: Brightman J held that a professional trustee company must monitor the activities of companies in which the trust holds a controlling interest and must take steps to prevent ultra vires or imprudent activities by company management. The standard of care for a professional trustee is higher than that of a lay trustee; a corporate trustee must exercise the care and skill of a professional in the same business. The case established the 'Bartlett duty' to supervise company investments.
key_quote: A corporate trustee holding a controlling interest in a company must monitor its activities and intervene to prevent imprudent or ultra vires acts; the standard expected of a professional trustee is higher than that of an ordinary person.
full_summary: Bartlett v Barclays Bank Trust Co Ltd concerned a trust which held approximately 99.8% of the shares in a private property company, BTH. Barclays Bank, as trustee, failed to monitor the company's activities and was not informed of or did not act upon two speculative property development schemes entered into by BTH management. One scheme, the Old Bailey development, resulted in very substantial losses. Brightman J held that Barclays was in breach of trust. A trustee holding a controlling interest in a company is not entitled to sit back and act merely as a passive shareholder; it must take active steps to keep informed about the company's activities, attend board meetings if necessary, and intervene to prevent the company from engaging in imprudent or unauthorised activities. Furthermore, the standard of care applicable to a professional corporate trustee is higher than that required of an ordinary lay trustee: the bank was required to exercise the skill and care of a professional trust corporation. The judgment is the leading authority on the monitoring duties of professional trustees with controlling shareholdings and the elevated standard of care expected of professional trustees.

court: England and Wales Court of Appeal
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal established the original Hastings-Bass rule: where trustees exercise a discretion without taking into account considerations which they ought to have taken into account (or taking into account irrelevant ones), the exercise is voidable, not void. The rule provided a broad ground for setting aside trustee decisions. This rule was later significantly restricted by the Supreme Court in Pitt v Holt [2013].
key_quote: Where trustees exercise a discretion without considering all relevant factors, the exercise may be voidable at the instance of the beneficiaries if the decision would have been different had the relevant factors been considered.
full_summary: Re Hastings-Bass gave its name to one of the most significant equitable doctrines in modern trust law. The case itself concerned the exercise by trustees of a power of advancement under a settlement in favour of a beneficiary, which had unintended tax consequences. The Court of Appeal held that where trustees exercise a discretionary power without taking into account all the factors they ought to have considered, or by taking into account irrelevant factors, the purported exercise of that power is voidable at the instance of the beneficiaries. The court stated that a trustee's discretion must be genuinely and properly exercised, and that an exercise that is fundamentally flawed because of an error in the deliberative process may be set aside by the court. In the decades following, the rule was applied very broadly by courts — particularly in tax-planning contexts — to set aside trustee decisions that had produced unforeseen adverse tax consequences. The full scope and proper basis of the rule was ultimately reconsidered and substantially curtailed by the Supreme Court in Pitt v Holt [2013] UKSC 26.

legal_principle: The Supreme Court (Lord Walker) significantly restricted the Hastings-Bass rule, holding that a trustee decision will only be set aside for inadequate deliberation where the trustee was acting in breach of duty — not merely where they made a decision they would have made differently with full information. The court also addressed the circumstances in which equity will set aside a transaction for mistake, distinguishing between mistakes as to effect (sufficient) and mistakes as to motive or consequences (generally not).
key_quote: The Hastings-Bass rule only operates where trustees acted in breach of duty; it does not permit beneficiaries to set aside every decision made on the basis of incomplete information.
full_summary: Pitt v Holt was a consolidated appeal decided by the Supreme Court, which took the opportunity to examine and restate the proper scope of the Hastings-Bass rule and the equitable jurisdiction to set aside transactions for mistake. Lord Walker, delivering the leading judgment, held that the rule in Re Hastings-Bass had been applied too broadly in subsequent decisions. The rule does not operate simply because trustees made a decision that they would have made differently had they been fully informed. Rather, the rule only applies where the trustees were in breach of their fiduciary duty in the deliberative process — for example, where they failed to obtain proper advice that they were required to obtain, or where they acted on fundamentally incorrect information. The court also considered the equitable jurisdiction to set aside voluntary dispositions for mistake, holding that equity will grant relief where there is a serious mistake as to the effect of the transaction (what the transaction actually does) — but not merely for mistakes as to collateral consequences such as tax liabilities, which are regarded as mistakes of motive rather than effect. The decision fundamentally reshaped the law on trustee decision-making and tax-based trust rescission.

case_name: Abacus Trust Co (Isle of Man) Ltd v Barr
legal_principle: The court applied the pre-Pitt v Holt version of the Hastings-Bass rule to hold that a trustee's appointment of funds under a discretionary trust was voidable where the trustee had not been informed of the adverse tax consequences of the appointment. The case is a leading pre-Pitt v Holt example of the broad application of the rule in tax-planning contexts, and illustrates the type of cases now subject to the more restrictive Pitt v Holt analysis.
key_quote: A trustee who makes an appointment without being informed of its tax consequences may have the appointment set aside under the Hastings-Bass rule where the trustee would have acted differently had they been properly advised.
full_summary: Abacus Trust Co (Isle of Man) Ltd v Barr is a significant pre-Pitt v Holt authority on the application of the Hastings-Bass rule. The case involved an appointment of funds from a discretionary trust which had adverse and unintended inheritance tax consequences. The trustee had not been properly advised about those consequences before making the appointment. Applying the broad version of the Hastings-Bass rule as it then stood, the court held that the appointment was voidable because the trustee had exercised its discretion without taking into account the relevant consideration of the tax consequences. Had the trustee been properly advised, it would not have made the appointment in that form. Lightman J's judgment articulated the mechanics of the rule carefully: the court must ask whether the trustee failed to take into account a relevant consideration, and whether the decision would have been different had that consideration been taken into account. Post-Pitt v Holt, this case would be analysed differently: it would be necessary to show that the trustee was in breach of duty in failing to obtain the relevant advice, not merely that the outcome would have been different. The case remains important as an illustration of the pre-2013 approach.

legal_principle: Lloyd J applied and articulated the (pre-Pitt v Holt) Hastings-Bass rule in detail, holding that trustee decisions made without consideration of all relevant factors are voidable. The case provided important guidance on the distinction between void and voidable acts under the rule and addressed the question of whether the rule could operate to set aside professional advice given to trustees.
key_quote: Where trustees exercise a power without taking into account a relevant consideration or taking into account an irrelevant one, the exercise is voidable and may be set aside by the court.
full_summary: Sieff v Fox is an important pre-Pitt v Holt authority in which Lloyd J undertook a comprehensive review of the Hastings-Bass rule and its scope. The case concerned a restructuring of a family trust which had been advised upon by solicitors but which produced adverse and unintended capital gains tax consequences. The trustees argued that their decision should be set aside under Hastings-Bass because they had not been advised of the tax effects. Lloyd J held that the rule applied: the trustees had exercised a power without considering a relevant factor (the tax consequences), and the exercise was voidable. The judgment also addressed the important but difficult question of whether the trustee act is void or merely voidable — concluding it is voidable, and that the court has a discretion whether to set it aside. Lloyd J also examined the relationship between the Hastings-Bass rule and the equitable jurisdiction to set aside a transaction for mistake, noting that they overlap but are not identical. Following Pitt v Holt, the analysis in Sieff v Fox requires modification: a breach of duty by the trustee in the deliberative process is now required.

legal_principle: The court construed a trust deed containing broad investment powers as authorising the trustees to invest outside England, despite the absence of any express authority to do so. The case illustrates the general rule that investment clauses in trust deeds are construed liberally in favour of the trustees, provided the investments are of the type that a reasonable business person would make with funds of their own.
key_quote: A broad investment clause in a trust deed should be construed liberally and as authorising investments that a reasonable business person would make with their own funds, unless expressly restricted.
full_summary: In re Harari's Settlement Trusts concerned the construction of an investment clause in a marriage settlement which authorised the trustees to invest 'in or upon such investments as to them may seem fit'. The question was whether this wording authorised investment in securities outside England and Wales. Jenkins J held that such broadly worded investment clauses should be given their natural and liberal meaning: the clause entitled the trustees to invest in any investment that a reasonable and prudent business person would consider appropriate for investment of personal funds, without geographical restriction unless the deed imposed one expressly. The case established an important principle of construction for investment clauses: broad and general language should not be read restrictively simply because it does not expressly authorise a particular type of investment. This approach complemented the development of the prudent man of business standard from Learoyd v Whiteley and foreshadowed the wider investment powers subsequently conferred by statute under the Trustee Act 2000. The case is regularly cited in discussions of express investment powers and their interaction with the statutory framework.

legal_principle: The Supreme Court of Ireland considered the standard of care applicable to trustees in the management and investment of trust property. The court confirmed that Irish equity applies the prudent man of business standard derived from Learoyd v Whiteley and that a trustee must balance the interests of income and capital beneficiaries when making investment decisions. The case affirms the continuing relevance of the English trustee investment principles in Irish law.
key_quote: Irish trustees must exercise the standard of care of a prudent man of business when managing trust investments, balancing the interests of income and capital beneficiaries.
full_summary: Stacey v Branch is an important Irish authority confirming the standard of care expected of trustees in the management and investment of trust assets. The Supreme Court of Ireland considered allegations that a trustee had failed to manage and invest trust property with the requisite degree of care and skill. The court confirmed that Irish equity follows the English prudent man of business standard as articulated by Lord Watson in Learoyd v Whiteley (1887): a trustee must take the same care as an ordinary prudent man of business would take in managing his own affairs of a similar kind. The court also confirmed that trustees in Ireland must give proper weight to balancing the interests of income beneficiaries (who require income from the fund) and capital beneficiaries (who require preservation and growth of capital), and must not unduly favour one class over the other. The decision demonstrates the consistent approach of Irish courts in adopting and applying the English equitable principles on trustee investment as part of Irish trust law, while leaving open the possibility of divergence where Irish statute or constitutional considerations require.

legal_principle: The Irish High Court considered the rights of beneficiaries to obtain accounts and information from trustees. The court confirmed that beneficiaries of a trust are entitled to see trust accounts and to receive full and frank information about the trust fund and its management, subject to considerations of confidentiality in relation to other beneficiaries. The case illustrates the trustee's duty of disclosure in Irish trust law.
key_quote: Beneficiaries are entitled to receive full and accurate accounts and information from trustees about the trust fund and its management; this right of disclosure is a fundamental incident of the trust relationship.
full_summary: Arnott v Arnott is a significant Irish High Court authority on the duty of trustees to provide accounts and information to beneficiaries. The case arose from a family dispute in which beneficiaries sought access to trust accounts, correspondence, and other documentation relating to the management of a family trust. The court confirmed that the right of a beneficiary to inspect and receive trust accounts is a fundamental aspect of the trust relationship: it is through access to accounts and information that beneficiaries are able to monitor the management of the trust fund and to identify any breach of duty by the trustees. The court recognised that this right is not absolute and may be subject to qualification where, for example, disclosure would prejudice the confidentiality interests of other beneficiaries, or where information is privileged. However, such qualifications are narrow and the default position strongly favours disclosure. The decision reflects and applies the broader principle established in English equity — most authoritatively in Schmidt v Rosewood Trust Ltd [2003] — that the court has an inherent supervisory jurisdiction over trusts and will ensure beneficiaries receive sufficient information to protect their interests.

case_name: Special Commissioners of Income Tax v Pemsel
legal_principle: Lord Macnaghten classified charitable purposes into four categories: (1) relief of poverty; (2) advancement of education; (3) advancement of religion; and (4) other purposes beneficial to the community. This Pemsel classification dominated charitable trust law for over a century and, while the Charities Act 2009 in Ireland and the Charities Act 2011 in England have expanded the list of recognised charitable purposes, the Pemsel categories remain foundational.
key_quote: Charity in its legal sense comprises four principal divisions: trusts for the relief of poverty, trusts for the advancement of education, trusts for the advancement of religion, and trusts for other purposes beneficial to the community.
full_summary: Special Commissioners of Income Tax v Pemsel is one of the most important cases in the law of charitable trusts. The case arose from a dispute about whether a trust for the advancement of missionary work was entitled to income tax exemption on the basis that it was charitable. In his celebrated speech, Lord Macnaghten set out the four-fold classification of charitable purposes that bears his name: relief of poverty, advancement of education, advancement of religion, and other purposes beneficial to the community. This classification — the 'Pemsel heads' — became the organising framework for charitable trust law in England and Ireland for more than a century. Every purpose claimed to be charitable had to be brought within one of the four heads. The fourth head, 'purposes beneficial to the community', has always required that the purpose be analogous to purposes within the other three heads or to existing recognised charitable purposes (the 'spirit and intendment' of the preamble to the Statute of Charitable Uses 1601). The Pemsel classification has now been superseded in England by the Charities Act 2011, which recognises thirteen heads, and in Ireland by the Charities Act 2009, which sets out its own list of charitable purposes. However, Pemsel remains the essential foundation for understanding the historical development of charitable trust law.

court: England and Wales Court of Appeal
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal held that an educational trust for the descendants of three named individuals was not charitable because the beneficiaries were defined by a personal nexus (family connection) rather than public benefit.
