Pennycuick J held that in such circumstances the court could rectify the document even though the mistake was not mutual. This decision has been followed in subsequent cases and represents an important qualification to the general rule that rectification requires a common mistake shared by both parties. It reflects equity's broader concern with unconscionable conduct and the principle that a party should not be permitted to take advantage of the other's known error. The case is frequently cited alongside Thomas Bates & Son Ltd v Wyndham's and Joscelyne v Nissen as a leading authority on the equitable remedy of rectification.

case_name: Thomas Bates & Son Ltd v Wyndham's (Lingerie) Ltd
court: England and Wales Court of Appeal
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal confirmed that rectification requires proof that: (1) the parties had a prior common continuing intention; (2) there was an outward expression of that intention; (3) the instrument failed to give effect to that intention; and (4) it is just and equitable to order rectification. The case also confirms that rectification can be refused on discretionary grounds even where the strict requirements are met.
key_quote: Rectification requires a prior common continuing intention, an outward expression of that intention, a failure of the document to give effect to it, and that it be just and equitable to order rectification.
full_summary: Thomas Bates & Son Ltd v Wyndham's (Lingerie) Ltd [1981] 1 WLR 505 is a leading Court of Appeal authority on the requirements for the equitable remedy of rectification of a written contract. Buckley LJ, delivering the principal judgment, identified the four conditions that must be satisfied before rectification will be granted: first, that the parties had a prior common continuing intention with regard to the relevant provision; second, that there was an outward expression of that common intention; third, that the instrument as executed failed to give effect to that common intention; and fourth, that it is just and equitable in all the circumstances to order rectification. The court also confirmed that even where these conditions are met, rectification remains a discretionary remedy and may be refused if, for example, third party rights have intervened or the balance of justice does not favour the grant of the remedy. The case also touches on unilateral mistake rectification, confirming that where one party knew of the other's mistake it may be unconscionable to resist rectification. This authority is now the standard starting point for any analysis of rectification in English and Irish equity, and is cited routinely in examination questions and in practice.

court: England and Wales Court of Appeal
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal held that rectification of a written contract requires proof of a prior common continuing intention shared by both parties. It is not sufficient that one party had a different subjective intention; what matters is the parties' outward expression of their common intention up to the moment of contracting. The case confirmed that a prior concluded antecedent agreement is not strictly necessary — a continuing common intention evidenced by outward expression suffices.
key_quote: Rectification requires proof of a prior common continuing intention evidenced by outward expression; mere subjective intention of one party, however clearly held, does not suffice.
full_summary: Joscelyne v Nissen [1970] 2 QB 86 is a foundational Court of Appeal authority on the equitable remedy of rectification. The case arose from a family arrangement under which a father transferred his car-hire business to his daughter in exchange for her agreement to pay certain household bills. The written agreement failed to include the obligation to pay gas, electricity and coal bills, which the parties had clearly intended to include. The Court of Appeal held that rectification was available. Russell LJ, delivering the principal judgment, addressed the question of whether a prior concluded contract was necessary before rectification could be ordered. He held that a prior concluded contract was not strictly necessary; what was required was a prior common continuing intention, evidenced by some outward expression, which the written instrument failed to reflect. Purely subjective intention, no matter how clearly held by one party, is insufficient to found a claim for rectification. The decision is important because it clarifies the standard of proof required (convincing evidence beyond the balance of probabilities on some views, though the standard remains debated) and confirms that the focus is on the parties' shared, outwardly expressed intention. Joscelyne v Nissen is routinely cited as the leading English authority on the requirements for rectification of written instruments and is a standard examination authority.

legal_principle: Lord St Leonards LC held that while equity would not specifically enforce an opera singer's contract to perform at the plaintiff's opera house, it could grant an injunction restraining her from performing at a rival opera house in breach of a negative covenant in the contract. The court distinguished between enforcing positive obligations (not possible for personal service contracts) and restraining breach of negative stipulations (possible).
key_quote: Equity will not compel specific performance of a personal service contract, but it may restrain breach of a negative stipulation in such a contract by injunction.
full_summary: Lumley v Wagner (1852) 1 De GM & G 604 is one of the most celebrated cases in equity, establishing a distinction that remains central to the law of injunctions and specific performance to this day. The defendant, Johanna Wagner, a celebrated opera singer, had contracted to perform exclusively at the plaintiff's opera house for a specified period and had covenanted not to perform at any other establishment during that time. She subsequently agreed to perform at a rival opera house and sought to break her contract with the plaintiff. Lord St Leonards LC held that equity could not specifically enforce the positive obligation to perform, as that would involve compelling personal service, which is contrary to equitable principle. However, the negative covenant — not to perform elsewhere — was a separate and distinct obligation which the court could enforce by injunction. Granting an injunction to restrain the defendant from performing at the rival opera house did not indirectly compel her to perform for the plaintiff; she remained free to do other work or nothing at all. The case established the Lumley v Wagner principle: equity will not specifically enforce personal service contracts by positive order, but it can restrain breach of ancillary negative covenants. This principle was subsequently applied and extended in Warner Bros Pictures Inc v Nelson and remains a foundational authority on injunctions in personal service contexts.

case_name: Warner Bros Pictures Inc v Nelson
court: England and Wales King's Bench Division
legal_principle: Branson J followed Lumley v Wagner and granted an injunction restraining Bette Davis (contracted as an actress) from performing for another studio in breach of her exclusive service contract. The court confirmed that an injunction to restrain breach of a negative covenant in a personal service contract does not indirectly compel specific performance, as long as it does not leave the defendant with no reasonable means of earning a living other than performance of the contract.
key_quote: An injunction restraining breach of a negative covenant in a personal service contract is not an indirect enforcement of specific performance, provided it does not leave the defendant with no other means of employment.
full_summary: Warner Bros Pictures Inc v Nelson [1937] 1 KB 209 is a famous application and extension of the Lumley v Wagner principle in the context of the entertainment industry. The defendant was Bette Davis, a famous film actress who had entered into an exclusive service contract with Warner Bros. The contract included a negative covenant preventing her from performing for any other film studio or in any other entertainment medium during the contract period. Davis sought to break the contract and perform for other studios. Branson J granted an injunction to restrain Davis from performing for other studios in breach of her negative covenant. He applied the Lumley v Wagner principle and held that restraining the breach of a negative covenant was not the same as specifically enforcing the positive obligation to perform. However, the court qualified the principle: the injunction would not be granted if it effectively left the defendant with no reasonable means of earning a livelihood other than by returning to work for the plaintiff, as that would amount to indirect compulsion to specifically perform. On the facts, Davis had skills employable in other spheres and was not left without alternative means of income. The case is an essential authority alongside Lumley v Wagner and is regularly examined in the context of the limits of specific performance and the availability of injunctions to restrain breach of negative covenants.

legal_principle: The House of Lords held that a mandatory injunction (requiring a defendant to take positive steps to remedy a wrong) will only be granted where: (1) the plaintiff demonstrates a strong probability of future damage; (2) the damage would be very substantial; and (3) damages would be inadequate. Lord Upjohn stated that the court has an unfettered discretion and must weigh the injustice of granting the order against the injustice of refusing it.
key_quote: A mandatory injunction will be granted only where there is a strong probability of future substantial damage for which damages would be an inadequate remedy; the court weighs the injustice of granting or refusing the order.
full_summary: Redland Bricks Ltd v Morris [1970] AC 652 is the leading House of Lords authority on the criteria for the grant of a mandatory injunction, and in particular a quia timet mandatory injunction. The plaintiffs' market garden was damaged by subsidence caused by the defendants' excavation operations on neighbouring land. The plaintiffs sought a mandatory injunction requiring the defendants to restore support to their land. The House of Lords, in a speech by Lord Upjohn, set out the principles governing the grant of mandatory injunctions. The court must be satisfied that: first, there is a very strong probability that grave damage will occur in the future if the injunction is not granted; second, that damages would be an inadequate remedy; and third, that the defendant has acted wantonly and quite unreasonably in relation to its obligations. Lord Upjohn emphasised that the court has an unfettered discretion and must weigh the injustice of granting the mandatory order — which may impose an oppressive burden on the defendant — against the injustice of refusing it and leaving the plaintiff without adequate relief. On the facts, the House of Lords discharged the mandatory injunction because the cost of compliance would have been grossly disproportionate to the benefit to the plaintiff. The case is a standard authority on mandatory injunctions and is frequently examined alongside quia timet injunctions.

legal_principle: The House of Lords held, in exceptional circumstances, that a court may award an account of profits for breach of contract where the claimant has a legitimate interest in preventing the defendant's profit-making activity and all other remedies are inadequate. The case concerned a former spy who published his memoirs in breach of a contractual duty of confidentiality. Account of profits as a remedy for breach of contract is an exceptional remedy available only in rare cases.
key_quote: In exceptional circumstances, a court may award an account of profits for breach of contract where the claimant has a legitimate interest in preventing the defendant's gain and other remedies are inadequate.
full_summary: Attorney General v Blake [2001] 1 AC 268 is a landmark House of Lords decision that fundamentally altered the landscape of remedies for breach of contract by recognising, for the first time, that an account of profits can be awarded for breach of contract in truly exceptional circumstances. The defendant, George Blake, was a former member of the British Secret Intelligence Service who had been convicted of spying for the Soviet Union. On release from prison he published his memoirs, receiving a substantial advance, in breach of contractual undertakings not to disclose confidential information. The Crown sought an account of profits representing Blake's gains from the publication. Lord Nicholls, delivering the principal speech, accepted that the traditional view confined remedies for breach of contract to compensation for loss. However, he held that in truly exceptional cases — where the claimant has a legitimate interest in preventing the defendant from profiting from a breach and compensatory and other remedies are clearly inadequate — equity can award an account of profits. The case was regarded as exceptional because of the nature of Blake's obligations and the complete inadequacy of any other remedy. The case is frequently examined in the context of restitutionary and gain-based remedies and the boundaries of contract law.

legal_principle: Lord Hardwicke LC held that equity can act on the person of a defendant who is within its jurisdiction even in relation to property situated abroad, by ordering specific performance of a contract relating to foreign land. This established the foundational equitable maxim 'equity acts in personam' — equity operates on the conscience of the individual, not directly on the property itself, enabling the court to act wherever it has personal jurisdiction.
key_quote: Equity acts in personam; the court has jurisdiction to order a person present within the jurisdiction to specifically perform a contract relating to land abroad by acting on their conscience.
full_summary: Penn v Lord Baltimore (1750) 1 Ves Sen 444 is an eighteenth-century Court of Chancery decision of enduring constitutional and doctrinal significance. The case arose from a boundary dispute between the proprietors of Pennsylvania and Maryland, both of whom were present in England. Lord Hardwicke LC held that the Court of Chancery had jurisdiction to order specific performance of a contract relating to land in North America, notwithstanding that the land was not situated within the territorial jurisdiction of the English courts. The basis for this jurisdiction was the foundational equitable maxim that equity acts in personam: it does not purport to act directly upon property but rather upon the conscience of the defendant who is personally present within the jurisdiction. Because the defendant was present in England and subject to the personal jurisdiction of the court, the Chancellor could compel him in conscience to perform his obligations, regardless of where the relevant property was located. This maxim — equity acts in personam — is of central importance in the study of equity, explaining both the historical basis of the Court of Chancery's jurisdiction and the mechanism by which equitable remedies such as specific performance, injunctions and account of profits operate today. The case is a standard citation for the in personam nature of equitable remedies and features in all major equity textbooks.

legal_principle: The Supreme Court of Ireland considered the circumstances in which specific performance of a contract for the sale of land would be refused. The court confirmed that specific performance is a discretionary remedy and that the exercise of the discretion is governed by established equitable principles, including the consideration of hardship, laches, and the adequacy of damages. The case affirms that Irish courts follow the English equitable approach to specific performance.
key_quote: Specific performance is a discretionary remedy; the court will refuse it where damages are an adequate remedy or where hardship or unconscionability make it inequitable to compel performance.
full_summary: Lynch v Burke [1995] 2 IR 159 is a Supreme Court of Ireland decision addressing the circumstances in which the discretionary remedy of specific performance of a contract for the sale of land may be refused. The case affirms that Irish equity follows the established English equitable framework in relation to specific performance, treating the remedy as discretionary rather than automatic. The Supreme Court confirmed that specific performance will generally be granted for contracts relating to land, where the uniqueness of the property makes damages an inadequate substitute, but the court retains a broad discretion to refuse relief in appropriate circumstances. The relevant factors the court will consider include: whether damages would be an adequate remedy; whether the conduct of the plaintiff disentitles them to relief (including laches and delay); whether compelling performance would cause undue or unreasonable hardship to the defendant or third parties; and whether the overall circumstances make it inequitable or unconscionable to compel performance. The case illustrates that even in relation to land contracts — where specific performance is the primary remedy — the Irish courts will not grant the remedy mechanically but will exercise a genuine discretion guided by equitable principles. The decision is a useful Irish authority confirming the alignment of Irish and English equity in the law of specific performance and the central role of unconscionability and discretion in equitable jurisprudence.

case_name: American Cyanamid Co v Ethicon Ltd
legal_principle: Lord Diplock laid down the modern framework for interlocutory injunctions: (1) the claimant must show a serious question to be tried (not a prima facie case); (2) if damages would be an adequate remedy, no injunction; (3) if not, the court assesses the balance of convenience including the strength of respective cases; (4) if in doubt, maintain the status quo. This replaced the old prima facie case test and remains the foundational framework in English law.
key_quote: The threshold for an interlocutory injunction is not a prima facie case but whether there is a serious question to be tried; the court then balances convenience and assesses the adequacy of damages.
full_summary: American Cyanamid Co v Ethicon Ltd [1975] AC 396 is the landmark House of Lords decision that fundamentally reformed the law of interlocutory injunctions in England and Wales. Before American Cyanamid, the claimant was required to establish a prima facie case — a reasonably arguable case on the merits — before the court would consider granting interlocutory relief. Lord Diplock, delivering the principal speech, swept away this requirement and replaced it with a sequential test. First, the claimant must satisfy the court that there is a serious question to be tried: the claim must not be frivolous or vexatious, but there is no requirement to show a prima facie case on the merits. Second, the court asks whether damages would be an adequate remedy for the claimant if the injunction were refused and the claimant ultimately succeeded at trial. If damages would be adequate, no injunction should issue. Third, if damages would not be adequate for the claimant, the court considers whether an undertaking in damages given by the claimant would adequately protect the defendant if the injunction were granted and the defendant ultimately succeeded. Finally, if the matter remains in doubt, the court should favour the course of action that preserves the status quo pending trial. The case is now cited in every jurisdiction that has adopted the English equitable tradition and is the essential starting point for any analysis of interlocutory injunctions.

case_name: Campus Oil Ltd v Minister for Industry and Energy (No 2)
legal_principle: The Irish Supreme Court (O'Higgins CJ) modified the American Cyanamid test for Ireland, requiring that the plaintiff show not merely a serious question but a 'fair bona fide question' to be tried, and that the balance of convenience favours the grant. The Campus Oil test remains the foundational framework for interlocutory injunctions in Irish courts. The court confirmed that, while similar to American Cyanamid, the Irish test preserves a more traditional assessment of the plaintiff's prospects of success at the balance of convenience stage.
key_quote: For an interlocutory injunction in Ireland, the plaintiff must establish a fair bona fide question to be tried and demonstrate that the balance of convenience favours the grant.
full_summary: Campus Oil Ltd v Minister for Industry and Energy (No 2) [1983] IR 88 is the foundational Irish Supreme Court decision on the test for interlocutory injunctions in Irish law, and represents the principal adaptation and modification of the English American Cyanamid framework for the Irish legal system. The case arose from a challenge by independent oil distributors to a statutory scheme requiring them to purchase a quota of their oil requirements from the state-owned oil refinery at government-fixed prices. The plaintiffs sought an interlocutory injunction to restrain enforcement of the scheme pending trial. O'Higgins CJ, delivering the judgment of the Supreme Court, adopted the American Cyanamid approach in modified form. The Irish test requires the plaintiff to show a 'fair bona fide question' to be tried — a formulation that, while broadly similar to the 'serious question' standard, was understood by subsequent Irish courts to involve a somewhat more substantive assessment of the merits than Lord Diplock's formulation in American Cyanamid. The court then applies the balance of convenience test, considering adequacy of damages for both sides and which course of action is less likely to cause irreparable harm. Campus Oil became the definitive Irish test and was applied in every interlocutory injunction application until supplemented and refined by the Supreme Court in Merck Sharp & Dohme v Clonmel Healthcare [2019] IESC 65.

case_name: Merck Sharp & Dohme Corporation v Clonmel Healthcare Ltd
legal_principle: The Supreme Court (O'Donnell J) updated and refined the Irish framework for interlocutory injunctions. The court identified three distinct questions: (1) Is there a fair or serious question to be tried? (2) Could damages adequately compensate the plaintiff if they succeed? (3) Does the balance of justice (or 'balance of convenience') favour the grant? O'Donnell J clarified that the strength of each party's case may be considered at the balance of justice stage and that the status quo is not an overriding factor. This is now the primary Irish authority on interlocutory injunctions.
key_quote: The Irish test for interlocutory injunctions requires: a serious question to be tried; consideration of the adequacy of damages; and an assessment of the balance of justice, which may include the strength of each party's case.
full_summary: Merck Sharp & Dohme Corporation v Clonmel Healthcare Ltd [2019] IESC 65 is the most significant Irish Supreme Court decision on the law of interlocutory injunctions in the modern era, updating and refining the Campus Oil framework that had governed Irish practice since 1983. O'Donnell J, delivering the judgment of the Supreme Court, undertook a comprehensive review of the principles applicable to interlocutory injunctions in Ireland, placing the Campus Oil test in its proper context and clarifying a number of aspects of the law that had become uncertain. The court confirmed that the first step remains whether there is a fair or serious question to be tried, but clarified that this threshold is not set so low as to be meaningless. The second stage — the adequacy of damages — must be assessed bi-directionally: the court must consider whether damages would adequately compensate the plaintiff if the injunction is refused and they succeed at trial, and whether the defendant would be adequately compensated by the plaintiff's undertaking in damages if the injunction is granted but the defendant succeeds at trial. At the third stage — the balance of justice — the court confirmed that it may take into account the relative strength of each party's case on the merits, contrary to a strict reading of American Cyanamid. The court also confirmed that maintaining the status quo, while an important consideration, is not a trump card. Merck Sharp & Dohme is now the primary Irish authority on interlocutory injunctions and is cited in every significant application.

court: England and Wales Court of Appeal
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal held that a court will not grant an interlocutory injunction to restrain publication of allegedly defamatory material where the defendant intends to plead justification (truth) at trial, unless the plaintiff can show that the defence is clearly untenable. Bonnard v Perryman establishes the high threshold for prior restraint of alleged defamatory publications, reflecting the freedom of expression principle that the defendant should be allowed to ventilate their defence at a full trial.
key_quote: An interlocutory injunction to restrain publication of alleged defamation will not be granted where the defendant intends to plead justification, unless that defence is manifestly and certainly untenable.
full_summary: Bonnard v Perryman [1891] 2 Ch 269 is the leading English authority on the availability of prior restraint injunctions in defamation cases and establishes a principle of profound importance for the relationship between equity and freedom of expression. The plaintiff sought an interlocutory injunction to restrain the publication of allegedly libellous material. The defendant indicated an intention to plead justification — that is, that the published statements were true — as a defence at trial. The Court of Appeal held that in such circumstances the court should not grant a prior restraint injunction except in the clearest cases where the defence of justification is plainly bound to fail. The underlying rationale is that prior restraint of publication represents a serious interference with freedom of speech and that the interests of freedom of expression require that a defendant who asserts that his statements are true should be permitted to ventilate that defence before a jury at trial rather than be silenced by interlocutory order. The principle in Bonnard v Perryman was subsequently enshrined in statutory form in the United Kingdom by section 12 of the Human Rights Act 1998 and has been applied in the Irish context in cases such as Cogley v RTE and Connolly v RTE. It represents a specific and high threshold exception to the ordinary American Cyanamid / Campus Oil framework and is a standard examination authority.

legal_principle: The Irish High Court considered an application to restrain a broadcast by RTE on grounds of alleged defamation. The court applied the Bonnard v Perryman principle and held that the threshold for a prior restraint injunction in defamation cases is extremely high in Ireland; the court will not restrain publication except where the plaintiff can show the publication would inevitably be defamatory and no possible defence is available.
key_quote: Irish courts apply an exceptionally high threshold for prior restraint injunctions in defamation cases; publication will only be restrained where it is absolutely clear the material is defamatory and no defence is available.
full_summary: Cogley v RTE [2005] 4 IR 79 is a significant Irish High Court decision applying the Bonnard v Perryman prior restraint principle in the context of Irish broadcast media. The plaintiff sought an interlocutory injunction to restrain RTE from broadcasting a programme alleged to be defamatory of the plaintiff. The court was required to consider whether the ordinary Campus Oil framework applied or whether the special Bonnard v Perryman rule for defamation cases governed the application. The court held that the Bonnard v Perryman principle applies in Ireland in defamation cases involving prior restraint and that the threshold for granting such an injunction is extremely high. The rationale is two-fold: first, the constitutional protection of freedom of expression in Ireland under Article 40.6.1 of the Constitution; and second, the principle that a defendant who asserts a defence of truth or honest opinion (formerly justification or fair comment) should be allowed to advance that defence at trial rather than be prevented from speaking by interlocutory order. The court held that a prior restraint injunction in a defamation case will only be granted where it is absolutely clear that the publication is defamatory and that no possible defence is available. The case is an important Irish authority applying the Bonnard v Perryman principle in a constitutional and statutory context.

legal_principle: The Irish High Court refused an interlocutory injunction to restrain a television broadcast alleged to be defamatory, applying the Bonnard v Perryman principle in the Irish context. The court held that the constitutional protection of freedom of expression and the media's right to comment on matters of public interest require courts to be cautious in granting prior restraint injunctions against broadcasters, and that only in the clearest cases will such relief be granted.
key_quote: The constitutional right to freedom of expression and the public interest in broadcasting require courts to apply a very high threshold before granting prior restraint injunctions against media defendants.
full_summary: Connolly v RTE [2001] 4 IR 61 is a High Court of Ireland decision in which the court refused to grant an interlocutory injunction restraining a television broadcast on grounds of alleged defamation. The case predates Cogley v RTE and is an earlier application of the Bonnard v Perryman prior restraint principle in the Irish context. The plaintiff sought to restrain a broadcast that allegedly contained defamatory material about the plaintiff. The court applied the principle that a prior restraint injunction in a defamation case will not be granted where the defendant asserts a defence that is not manifestly untenable. The court placed particular weight on the constitutional dimension of the case: Article 40.6.1 of the Irish Constitution expressly protects the right to free expression of convictions and opinions, and the court emphasised that this right is of particular importance in relation to broadcasting media that serve a vital public function in informing the citizenry on matters of public interest. The court held that prior restraint of a broadcast could only be justified in the clearest possible cases where no reasonable defence was available to the broadcaster. The case is routinely cited alongside Cogley v RTE as an Irish authority on the high threshold for defamation-based prior restraint injunctions and the constitutional dimension of freedom of expression in Irish equity.

legal_principle: The High Court granted an interlocutory injunction to reinstate an employee who had been suspended without fair procedures, applying the Campus Oil test. The court held that the balance of convenience favoured the grant of the injunction because the plaintiff had an arguable case that the suspension was unlawful and damages would not adequately compensate for the continuing damage to the plaintiff's professional reputation during the period of unlawful suspension.
key_quote: An interlocutory injunction may be granted to reinstate an employee unlawfully suspended where damages would be an inadequate remedy for the damage to professional reputation and career.
full_summary: Lingham v Health Services Executive [2006] ELR 137 is a High Court of Ireland decision of practical importance in the employment law context, illustrating the application of the Campus Oil interlocutory injunction test in cases involving the suspension of employees. The plaintiff, a medical professional, had been suspended by the defendant health authority pending investigation of disciplinary matters. The plaintiff challenged the suspension on grounds of a failure to comply with fair procedures and applied for an interlocutory injunction requiring reinstatement pending trial. The court applied the Campus Oil test and held that the plaintiff had raised a fair bona fide question as to the legality of the suspension procedure. On the question of the adequacy of damages, the court held that damages would be an inadequate remedy because the damage to the plaintiff's professional reputation and career caused by a period of unlawful suspension was not readily quantifiable in monetary terms and could not be fully compensated by an award of damages after the event. The court held that the balance of convenience accordingly favoured granting the injunction. The case is significant because it confirms that damage to professional reputation can render damages an inadequate remedy in the employment context and that the courts will grant interlocutory mandatory relief requiring reinstatement where the suspension is arguably unlawful and the harm is irreparable.

case_name: Maha Lingham v Health Service Executive
legal_principle: The Supreme Court considered the criteria for granting an interlocutory mandatory injunction in an employment context, requiring reinstatement of an employee to their position pending trial. The court confirmed that the threshold for a mandatory interlocutory injunction is higher than for a prohibitory one, as the court is not merely preserving the status quo but requiring positive action. The balance of convenience test applies, but with additional caution in mandatory injunction cases.
key_quote: The threshold for a mandatory interlocutory injunction is higher than for a prohibitory one; the plaintiff must show a strong case and that the balance of convenience clearly favours the grant.
full_summary: Maha Lingham v Health Service Executive [2005] IESC 89 is an important Supreme Court of Ireland decision addressing the specific and more demanding criteria that apply when a plaintiff seeks a mandatory interlocutory injunction — that is, an injunction requiring the defendant to take positive steps — as opposed to a prohibitory injunction that merely restrains the defendant from acting. The plaintiff, a medical professional, sought reinstatement to their employment position pending trial of an action challenging the termination or suspension of their employment. The Supreme Court confirmed that the threshold for granting a mandatory interlocutory injunction is higher than for a prohibitory interlocutory injunction. Where a court grants a prohibitory injunction, it is preserving the existing state of affairs pending trial; where it grants a mandatory injunction, it is requiring a defendant to take positive action and thereby potentially changing the legal or practical position in a way that may be difficult to reverse. For this reason, the court must be satisfied not merely that there is a fair bona fide question to be tried, but that the plaintiff has demonstrated a strong case likely to succeed at trial. The balance of convenience must also clearly and unequivocally favour the grant. The case is a leading Irish authority on mandatory interlocutory injunctions and is essential reading alongside Lingham v HSE and the Campus Oil line of authority.

case_name: Betty Martin Financial Services Ltd v EBS DAC
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal applied the updated Merck Sharp & Dohme framework to an application for an interlocutory injunction in a mortgage dispute. The court confirmed that the Merck test requires consideration of the strength of the plaintiff's case at the balance of justice stage and that the adequacy of damages must be assessed in relation to both parties. The case is a practical application of the updated Irish framework.
key_quote: The Merck test requires the court to consider the strength of each party's case at the balance of justice stage; the adequacy of damages must be assessed for both parties.
full_summary: Betty Martin Financial Services Ltd v EBS DAC [2019] IECA 327 is a Court of Appeal of Ireland decision that provides an important practical application of the updated Merck Sharp & Dohme framework for interlocutory injunctions in the context of a commercial mortgage dispute. The case is significant as one of the earliest Court of Appeal decisions to apply the Merck test following the Supreme Court's reformulation of the Irish framework in Merck Sharp & Dohme Corporation v Clonmel Healthcare Ltd [2019] IESC 65. The plaintiff sought an interlocutory injunction in the context of a dispute with a financial institution arising from a mortgage arrangement. The Court of Appeal applied the three-stage Merck framework and confirmed that at the balance of justice stage — the third stage — the court is entitled and required to consider the relative strength of each party's case on the merits of the underlying dispute, a significant departure from a strict reading of the American Cyanamid approach. The court also confirmed that the adequacy of damages assessment is bilateral: it is not sufficient to ask only whether damages would compensate the plaintiff; the court must also assess whether the defendant would be adequately protected by the plaintiff's undertaking in damages. The decision is a practical illustration of how the updated Merck framework operates in commercial litigation and confirms its application by the Court of Appeal.

legal_principle: The Supreme Court considered the criteria for interlocutory injunctions in Ireland before Campus Oil was decided. The court applied a prima facie case standard and emphasised the importance of the adequacy of damages in the balance of convenience analysis. The case is an early Irish authority on interlocutory injunctions and reflects the pre-Campus Oil approach that was subsequently superseded.
key_quote: Before Campus Oil, Irish courts required the plaintiff to establish a prima facie case and assessed whether damages would adequately compensate both parties in the balance of convenience analysis.
full_summary: Doran v Thompson & Sons Ltd [1978] IR 223 is an early Supreme Court of Ireland authority on the test for interlocutory injunctions in Irish law, predating the landmark Campus Oil decision of 1983 that transformed the Irish approach. At the time of Doran, the Irish courts had not yet definitively resolved whether to adopt the American Cyanamid framework (which had been decided by the House of Lords in 1975) or to maintain a more traditional prima facie case requirement. The Supreme Court in Doran applied a prima facie case standard, requiring the plaintiff to demonstrate that they had a reasonably arguable case on the merits before the court would proceed to consider the balance of convenience. The court also emphasised the importance of the adequacy of damages test in the balance of convenience analysis, asking whether damages would provide an adequate remedy for the harm that would be suffered by each party depending on whether the injunction was granted or refused. The case is of historical interest as it represents the pre-Campus Oil Irish approach and illustrates the evolution of Irish injunction law from a prima facie case requirement to the fair bona fide question standard adopted in Campus Oil and subsequently refined in Merck Sharp & Dohme. It is cited as a background authority in academic treatments of Irish interlocutory injunction law.

legal_principle: The High Court applied the Campus Oil test and considered the role of the status quo as a factor in the balance of convenience. The court confirmed that while maintaining the status quo is an important consideration, it is not a trump card; the court must assess the overall balance of justice having regard to the relative strength of the parties' cases and the consequences of granting or refusing relief.
key_quote: Maintaining the status quo is an important but not overriding consideration in the balance of convenience; the court must assess the overall balance of justice.
full_summary: Murphy v Grealish [2009] 3 IR 366 is a High Court of Ireland decision applying the Campus Oil test for interlocutory injunctions and addressing, in particular, the role and weight to be given to the status quo as a factor in the balance of convenience. The case considered the extent to which a court should be guided by a preference for maintaining the existing state of affairs when deciding whether to grant or refuse interlocutory relief. The court confirmed that the status quo is a relevant and in many cases important consideration in the balance of convenience analysis, consistent with the principle identified by Lord Diplock in American Cyanamid that when the balance of convenience is otherwise roughly equal the court should prefer the course that maintains the status quo pending trial. However, the court emphasised that the status quo is not a trump card that automatically resolves the balance of convenience in favour of the party seeking to preserve the existing state of affairs. The court must assess the overall balance of justice, taking into account all relevant factors including the relative strength of each party's case, the nature and extent of the harm that would be suffered by each party depending on the outcome of the application, and the availability of damages as an adequate remedy. The case is a useful illustration of the practical application of the Campus Oil framework and foreshadows the Merck Sharp & Dohme court's confirmation that the status quo is not an overriding factor.

case_name: Hartside Ltd v Heineken Ireland Ltd
legal_principle: The High Court applied the Campus Oil / Merck framework to a commercial dispute arising from the termination of a distribution agreement. The court found that damages would not be an adequate remedy because the plaintiff's business would be unable to survive the period before trial without the injunction. The case illustrates the application of the adequacy of damages test in a commercial context and the significance of business viability to the balance of convenience.
key_quote: Where refusal of an injunction would result in the plaintiff's business being unable to survive to trial, damages are not an adequate remedy and the balance of convenience favours the grant.
full_summary: Hartside Ltd v Heineken Ireland Ltd [2010] IEHC 3 is a High Court of Ireland decision providing a valuable illustration of the application of the Campus Oil interlocutory injunction framework in a commercial context, and in particular the assessment of the adequacy of damages in circumstances where the plaintiff's business viability is at stake. The case arose from the termination by Heineken Ireland of a distribution agreement with the plaintiff, a small distribution company whose business was almost entirely dependent on the Heineken distribution contract. The plaintiff sought an interlocutory injunction to restrain the termination pending the determination of proceedings challenging its validity. The court applied the Campus Oil test and found that the plaintiff raised a fair bona fide question as to the validity of the termination. On the adequacy of damages, the court held that damages would not be an adequate remedy because the termination of the distribution agreement would inevitably result in the collapse of the plaintiff's business before trial: the business could not survive the litigation period without the contract, and a later award of damages could not restore the business that would by then have ceased to exist. The court held that where refusal of the injunction would cause the destruction of the plaintiff's business, the adequacy of damages test strongly favours the plaintiff. The balance of convenience accordingly favoured granting the injunction. The case is a standard practical authority on business viability and the adequacy of damages in commercial injunction applications.

case_name: Anton Piller KG v Manufacturing Processes Ltd
legal_principle: The Anton Piller order (now called a search order) allows a plaintiff to enter the defendant's premises without prior notice to search for and seize evidence or infringing goods. Lord Denning MR held that such relief requires: (1) an extremely strong prima facie case; (2) actual or potential damage to the plaintiff that is very serious; and (3) clear evidence that the defendant possesses incriminating documents or items and would destroy or conceal them if given notice of the application. The order is a drastic remedy granted only in compelling circumstances.
key_quote: There is jurisdiction in the court to make such an order... when it is essential that the plaintiff should have inspection so that justice can be done between the parties.
full_summary:
Anton Piller KG, a German manufacturer of electrical equipment, suspected that its UK agent, Manufacturing Processes Ltd, was supplying confidential technical drawings to competitors. Fearing that evidence would be destroyed if notice were given, Anton Piller applied ex parte for an order permitting its solicitors to enter the defendant's premises and inspect and remove relevant documents and materials.

The Court of Appeal upheld the grant of such relief and, crucially, Lord Denning MR laid down the three conditions that must be satisfied before a without-notice search order can be made: first, the plaintiff must show an extremely strong prima facie case on the merits; second, the actual or potential damage to the plaintiff must be very serious; and third, there must be clear evidence that the defendant possesses incriminating documents or items and there is a real possibility that they will destroy them if forewarned.

Lord Denning emphasised that the order does not authorise forcible entry — the defendant may refuse entry, but does so at the risk of being in contempt of court. The order must be carried out with the utmost care to minimise any injustice to the defendant.

This case gave its name to a category of interim relief that has since been placed on a statutory footing in England and Wales by the Civil Procedure Rules (CPR Part 25) as a 'search order'. The conditions set by Lord Denning remain the baseline requirements in both English and Irish courts when such drastic without-notice relief is sought.

legal_principle: Scott J held that Anton Piller orders had been applied oppressively and in a manner that went far beyond what was justified, criticising the practice of seizing items without a proper schedule and without affording the defendant access to independent legal advice. The order must be drafted with precision; only items specifically described may be seized, and the defendant's solicitor should be present during execution to protect the defendant's interests. The court emphasised that such orders must not be used as instruments of oppression and that the duty of full and frank disclosure is strictly enforced.
key_quote: The practice of Anton Piller orders had been developing in a manner which gave the plaintiffs too much power and the defendants too little protection.
full_summary:
Columbia Pictures obtained Anton Piller orders against Robinson and others involved in alleged video piracy of copyright-protected films. When the orders were executed, the plaintiff's solicitors seized a very large quantity of materials, many of which fell outside the scope of what was authorised. The defendant subsequently challenged both the original grant and the manner of execution.

Scott J engaged in a wide-ranging review of the practice surrounding Anton Piller orders and found serious deficiencies. He criticised the tendency of applicants to over-seize materials without maintaining proper lists, to execute orders without giving the defendant an opportunity to consult a solicitor, and to treat the procedure as a fishing expedition rather than a targeted search for specified evidence.

Scott J laid down important procedural safeguards: all items seized must be listed at the time of seizure; copies should be left with the defendant; the defendant must be informed of the right to seek legal advice before permitting entry; a supervising solicitor independent of the plaintiff should ideally be present; and the order must only authorise inspection or removal of specifically described items.

This case is a landmark in the development of procedural safeguards around search orders. Many of Scott J's recommendations were subsequently incorporated into the standard form of search order under the Civil Procedure Rules. The case remains authority for the proposition that equity will not countenance the oppressive use of without-notice relief.

case_name: Microsoft Corporation v Brightpoint Ireland Ltd
legal_principle: The Irish High Court granted a search order (Anton Piller-type relief) against a defendant accused of large-scale software piracy, adopting the conditions established in Anton Piller KG v Manufacturing Processes Ltd as the applicable test in Irish law. The court emphasised that the remedy must be proportionate to the wrong alleged and that the plaintiff must give a full and frank account of all relevant facts on the without-notice application. The order was tailored to minimise disruption to the defendant's legitimate business activities while preserving the evidence sought.
key_quote: The court must be satisfied that there is a real possibility that the defendant would destroy or conceal the relevant materials if given advance notice of the application.
full_summary:
Microsoft Corporation applied without notice to the Irish High Court for a search order against Brightpoint Ireland Ltd, a technology distribution company suspected of distributing counterfeit or unlicensed versions of Microsoft software products on a significant commercial scale. Microsoft adduced evidence of test purchases and of the volume of infringing product allegedly in circulation.

The High Court granted the order, expressly confirming that the jurisdiction to make such relief exists in Irish law and that the appropriate conditions to be satisfied are those formulated by Lord Denning in Anton Piller KG v Manufacturing Processes Ltd: a strong prima facie case, serious potential damage, and clear evidence that relevant materials are held by the defendant and would be concealed or destroyed if advance notice were given.

The court added that the principle of proportionality is an important consideration in the Irish constitutional context: the order should be no wider than is strictly necessary to achieve its purpose, having regard to the interference with the defendant's property rights and right to conduct a business. Detailed provisions were included to protect the defendant's legitimate commercial documentation.

The case is significant as an Irish authority confirming the availability of search orders in intellectual property disputes and integrating the proportionality principle into the exercise of the jurisdiction, reflecting the influence of constitutional rights on equitable remedies in Irish law.

case_name: Mareva Compania Naviera SA v International Bulkcarriers SA
legal_principle: The Mareva injunction (now known as a freezing order) prevents a defendant from dissipating or removing assets from the jurisdiction so as to render any eventual judgment nugatory. Lord Denning MR held that the court has jurisdiction to restrain a defendant from dealing with assets in appropriate circumstances, and that two conditions must be met: the plaintiff must show a good arguable case on the merits, and there must be a real risk that the defendant will dissipate or remove assets before judgment can be enforced. The remedy operates in personam and does not create a proprietary interest in the frozen assets.
key_quote: If it appears that the debt is due and owing, and there is a danger that the debtor may dispose of his assets so as to defeat it before judgment, the court has jurisdiction to grant an interlocutory injunction so as to prevent him disposing of those assets.
full_summary:
Mareva Compania Naviera SA chartered a vessel to International Bulkcarriers SA under a charterparty. The charterers defaulted on hire payments, and freight money owed to them by a third party was due to be paid into a London bank account. Mareva feared that the charterers, who were foreign entities with no fixed presence in England, would remove the funds from the jurisdiction before judgment could be obtained and enforced.

The Court of Appeal, in an ex parte application, granted an injunction restraining the charterers from removing or disposing of the funds. Lord Denning MR held that the court possessed jurisdiction to freeze assets in these circumstances, drawing on the broad equitable jurisdiction to grant injunctions 'in all cases in which it appears just and convenient to do so' under what is now s 37 of the Senior Courts Act 1981.

The court identified the key requirements: the plaintiff must have a good arguable case on the substantive claim, and there must be a real risk of the defendant dissipating or removing assets so as to frustrate enforcement of any judgment. The plaintiff must also provide an undertaking in damages.

This decision established the Mareva injunction as a fundamental weapon in the creditor's armoury. It has since been codified in England and Wales as the 'freezing order' under CPR Part 25, and recognised in Irish law in Caudron v Air Zaire. It is one of the most significant procedural developments in twentieth-century commercial equity.

case_name: Z Ltd v A-Z and AA-LL
legal_principle: Lord Denning MR confirmed that the Mareva jurisdiction extends to the assets of a defendant located within the jurisdiction even where the defendant is domiciled or resident abroad. Once a third party such as a bank is given notice of a freezing order, that third party is bound by the order and must not assist the defendant in dealing with the frozen assets, on pain of contempt of court. The court also clarified that a freezing order does not give the plaintiff any proprietary interest or priority over other creditors in the frozen assets.
key_quote: The Mareva injunction extends to any assets of the defendant within the jurisdiction, no matter whether the defendant is within or without the jurisdiction.
full_summary:
Z Ltd sought to enforce a foreign arbitral award and applied for a Mareva injunction against foreign defendants who held assets in English bank accounts. The defendants were not resident in England and argued that the Mareva jurisdiction should not extend to defendants based abroad. The Court of Appeal rejected this argument.

Lord Denning MR held that the geographic location or residence of the defendant is irrelevant to the exercise of the Mareva jurisdiction; what matters is that the assets are within the territorial jurisdiction of the court. The plaintiff need only show a good arguable case and a real risk of dissipation, regardless of where the defendant is based.

The court also addressed the position of third parties, particularly banks holding the frozen funds. Once a bank or other third party receives notice of the freezing order, it is bound not to allow dealings with the frozen assets. A bank that permits the defendant to draw on frozen funds after receiving notice acts in contempt of court. The court set out practical guidance for the form and service of orders on banks.

Importantly, the Court of Appeal confirmed that a Mareva injunction creates no proprietary interest in favour of the plaintiff and confers no priority over the defendant's other creditors in the event of insolvency. Its sole purpose is to preserve the status quo pending judgment and enforcement.

This case significantly expanded the practical scope of Mareva relief and remains a leading authority on third-party obligations under freezing orders.

legal_principle: The Irish Supreme Court recognised the existence of Mareva jurisdiction in Ireland, holding that the courts have an inherent jurisdiction to restrain a defendant from removing or dissipating assets within the jurisdiction in order to prevent the frustration of a prospective judgment. O'Higgins CJ held that this jurisdiction derives from the court's inherent power to ensure the effective administration of justice and is not dependent on any statutory provision. The conditions of a good arguable case and a real risk of dissipation must both be satisfied.
key_quote: The courts have an inherent jurisdiction to restrain a defendant from removing assets from the jurisdiction where there is a real danger that a judgment, if obtained, would be rendered nugatory.
full_summary:
Caudron was owed money by Air Zaire and sought to recover a sum due under a contract. Fearing that Air Zaire would remove its assets from Ireland before judgment could be obtained, Caudron applied for a Mareva-type injunction to freeze those assets. The question for the Irish courts was whether the Mareva jurisdiction, which had been developed in English case law, was available in Ireland.

The Supreme Court, in a judgment delivered by O'Higgins CJ, held that the Irish courts possess an inherent jurisdiction to grant such relief. This jurisdiction is grounded in the courts' fundamental duty to ensure that their processes are not rendered futile by the conduct of litigants. It does not require statutory authority; it flows from the court's constitutional function under Article 34 of the Constitution.

O'Higgins CJ identified the conditions for the grant of the order: the plaintiff must show a strong prima facie case on the substantive claim, and there must be a real and demonstrable risk that the defendant will dissipate or remove assets from the jurisdiction so as to defeat enforcement of any judgment obtained.

This decision is the founding Irish authority for Mareva/freezing order jurisdiction and is routinely cited in subsequent Irish commercial litigation. The jurisdiction has since been confirmed and developed in a series of Irish High Court decisions, including Minister for Justice v Devine [2012] IEHC 326, extending it to the proceeds of crime context.

case_name: Independent Newspapers Ltd v Irish Press Ltd
legal_principle: The Irish High Court refused to grant an interlocutory injunction where the plaintiff had not established a strong prima facie case and where damages would be an adequate remedy. The court applied the balance of convenience test, holding that before granting interim relief a court must be satisfied both that there is a sufficiently strong case on the merits and that the inconvenience or prejudice to the plaintiff of refusing the injunction outweighs the prejudice to the defendant of granting it. Where damages are an adequate remedy, the court will generally decline to interfere by injunction.
key_quote: Where damages would adequately compensate the plaintiff for the injury complained of, an interlocutory injunction will not ordinarily be granted.
full_summary:
Independent Newspapers Ltd sought an interlocutory injunction against Irish Press Ltd, alleging that the defendant was engaged in conduct that interfered with the plaintiff's business interests. The precise nature of the alleged wrong related to competitive commercial practices in the newspaper industry.

The High Court refused the injunction on two independent grounds. First, the court was not satisfied that the plaintiff had established a strong prima facie case that it was entitled to the relief sought at full trial; the legal basis for the claim was uncertain and contested. Second, even if a prima facie case had been made out, the court held that any loss suffered by the plaintiff pending trial would be adequately compensable in damages, so that the granting of an injunction was not necessary to protect the plaintiff's position.

The court applied the balance of convenience, weighing the respective prejudices to the parties of granting or refusing the relief. In circumstances where the defendant was conducting a legitimate commercial business and damages could compensate the plaintiff, the balance did not favour intervention.

Although decided before the landmark decision in American Cyanamid Co v Ethicon Ltd [1975] AC 396, this case reflects the traditional Irish approach to interlocutory relief. It illustrates that the adequacy of damages is a threshold consideration and that courts are reluctant to interfere with ongoing commercial activities by injunction where monetary compensation is available.

legal_principle: The Irish Supreme Court granted an injunction restraining the publication of material alleged to be defamatory of the plaintiff, holding that a serious risk of defamatory publication can justify pre-trial restraint where the balance of convenience clearly favours the plaintiff. The court acknowledged the general reluctance to restrain publication before trial but held that this reluctance may be overcome where the defamatory character of the material is clear and the plaintiff would suffer irreparable damage to reputation that could not be adequately compensated in damages alone.
key_quote: In cases where publication of a libel would cause irreparable damage to a plaintiff's reputation, the balance of convenience may favour the grant of an injunction notwithstanding the general rule against prior restraint of publication.
full_summary:
Sinclair sought an injunction to prevent Gogarty from publishing a book that Sinclair contended contained defamatory material about him. Gogarty was a well-known Irish writer and the proposed publication was of a literary character. Sinclair argued that publication would cause serious and irreparable harm to his reputation.

The Supreme Court granted the injunction, accepting that the defamatory character of the material was sufficiently established at the interlocutory stage to justify restraint. The court weighed the competing interests: on one side, the plaintiff's right to protection of his reputation and the risk of irreparable damage; on the other, the defendant's right to publish and the principle that courts should be slow to engage in prior restraint of expression.

The court held that the balance of convenience clearly favoured the plaintiff in the circumstances, particularly given the irreversible nature of the harm to reputation once a defamatory publication is in circulation. Damages would not be an adequate remedy for the destruction of professional and personal reputation.

Sinclair v Gogarty is historically significant as an early Irish authority on injunctions in defamation cases. It is now read alongside later decisions such as Reynolds v Malocco [1999] 2 IR 203, which applied the Bonnard v Perryman rule restricting the availability of pre-publication injunctions in defamation actions.

legal_principle: The Irish High Court confirmed that the rule in Bonnard v Perryman applies in Ireland and severely restricts the availability of interlocutory injunctions to restrain allegedly defamatory publications. A court will not restrain publication pre-trial unless it is clear both that the material is defamatory and that the defendant could not succeed in any defence at trial, such as truth or fair comment. The freedom of the press and the public interest in free expression are powerful countervailing considerations that ordinarily outweigh a plaintiff's desire to prevent publication pending trial.
key_quote: The rule in Bonnard v Perryman is part of Irish law. A court will not restrain publication of an allegedly defamatory article unless it is clear that no defence could succeed at the trial of the action.
full_summary:
Reynolds, a well-known public figure, sought an interlocutory injunction to prevent the defendant magazine from publishing an article alleged to be defamatory of him. Reynolds argued that publication would cause serious reputational harm and that the material was clearly defamatory on its face.

The High Court refused the injunction. Kelly J held that the rule in Bonnard v Perryman [1891] 2 Ch 269 — which established that courts will not restrain the publication of allegedly defamatory material by injunction unless the defendant has no possible defence — forms part of Irish law. The rationale for this rule is the fundamental importance of freedom of expression and freedom of the press, which are protected both at common law and under the Irish Constitution (Article 40.6.1).

Kelly J held that where the defendant indicates an intention to justify the publication or to rely on fair comment or privilege, the court should not pre-empt the jury's determination of those issues by granting an injunction. To do so would be to substitute the court's view for the jury's on contested factual matters.

Reynolds v Malocco is the leading Irish authority on interlocutory injunctions in defamation proceedings. It establishes a high threshold for such relief and reflects the constitutional protection afforded to freedom of expression in Ireland, drawing on both common law tradition and the particular guarantee in Article 40.6.1.

case_name: Board of Management of Wilson's Hospital School v Enoch Burke
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal upheld the grant of a mandatory interlocutory injunction requiring a suspended teacher to cease attending school premises in defiance of his suspension, holding that a mandatory injunction is appropriate where the defendant's continuous conduct constitutes a serious and ongoing breach causing irreparable harm. The court applied the balance of justice test, finding that the school's need to maintain order and comply with its statutory obligations to staff and pupils far outweighed any inconvenience to the defendant. The case affirms that mandatory injunctive relief is available to enforce compliance with lawful directions even where the defendant asserts a principled objection to those directions.
key_quote: Where a defendant continues in breach of a court order in a manner that causes irreparable harm, a mandatory injunction may be the only effective remedy, and the balance of justice strongly favours its grant.
full_summary:
Enoch Burke, a teacher at Wilson's Hospital School, was suspended by the Board of Management following a disciplinary dispute. Despite his suspension and a court order requiring him to remain away from the school, Burke continued to attend the school premises on a daily basis, stating that he was acting on grounds of religious and professional principle. This conduct resulted in repeated findings of contempt of court and periods of imprisonment.

The Board of Management sought, and was granted, an interlocutory mandatory injunction by the High Court requiring Burke to cease attending school. Burke appealed, arguing inter alia that the injunction was disproportionate and that the threshold for a mandatory interlocutory injunction — which is higher than that for a prohibitory injunction — had not been met.

The Court of Appeal upheld the injunction. It held that the threshold for a mandatory interlocutory injunction, which requires the court to be satisfied that there is a strong case that the plaintiff will be entitled to the relief at full trial and that the balance of justice clearly favours the grant, was plainly satisfied. The ongoing nature of Burke's conduct was causing direct operational harm to the school's ability to manage its premises and staff, and no lesser remedy would be effective.

The case attracted significant public attention and is notable as a modern Irish authority on mandatory injunctions, confirming that equitable relief will be enforced even against a defendant who invokes conscience or belief as justification for continued breach.

case_name: Hoffmann-La Roche & Co AG v Secretary of State for Trade
legal_principle: The House of Lords held that when granting an interlocutory injunction, including a freezing order, the court must be satisfied that the plaintiff is in a position to honour the cross-undertaking in damages, which is the price the plaintiff pays for obtaining interim relief. Lord Diplock confirmed that the purpose of the cross-undertaking is to provide a mechanism by which the defendant may be compensated if the injunction was wrongly granted and the plaintiff fails at trial. The undertaking is therefore of practical significance and courts must have regard to the plaintiff's financial capacity to meet it.
key_quote: The grant of an interlocutory injunction is a discretionary remedy and the court requires an undertaking in damages so that, if the injunction proves to have been wrongly granted, the defendant can be adequately compensated.
full_summary:
Hoffmann-La Roche, a major pharmaceutical company, challenged government regulations requiring it to reduce the prices of its drugs Librium and Valium. The Secretary of State applied for an interlocutory injunction to restrain the company from charging prices in excess of the regulated level pending the outcome of the legal proceedings. The question arose as to the conditions upon which such relief should be granted, and in particular the significance of the cross-undertaking in damages.

The House of Lords, in a leading speech by Lord Diplock, analysed the basis on which interlocutory injunctions are granted and the role of the cross-undertaking in damages. Lord Diplock confirmed that the cross-undertaking is a standard and essential condition of interlocutory relief: the plaintiff must undertake to compensate the defendant for any losses caused by the injunction if it is ultimately found to have been wrongly granted.

The court must be satisfied that the plaintiff has the financial means to honour this undertaking; a hollow undertaking from an impecunious plaintiff provides no real protection for the defendant. This consideration is of heightened importance in the context of freezing orders, where the potential loss to the defendant from being unable to deal with frozen assets may be very substantial.

This decision predated American Cyanamid (decided the same year) and is regarded as a companion authority on the procedural mechanics of interlocutory injunctions. It is widely cited in applications for freezing orders and search orders where the capacity of the plaintiff to give a meaningful undertaking is in issue.

legal_principle: The Irish High Court granted a Mareva-type freezing order in the context of proceeds of crime proceedings, confirming that the Irish freezing jurisdiction extends beyond ordinary civil debt recovery to encompass assets suspected of representing the proceeds of criminal activity that are liable to confiscation. The court applied the principles established in Caudron v Air Zaire and confirmed that the broad inherent jurisdiction of the Irish courts to prevent the frustration of legal proceedings encompasses pre-confiscation asset freezing in the criminal law context.
key_quote: The court's inherent jurisdiction to restrain the dissipation of assets is not confined to civil debt recovery but extends to any situation where the removal or dissipation of assets would frustrate the court's process.
full_summary:
The Minister for Justice applied under the Proceeds of Crime Acts for an order freezing assets held by or on behalf of the respondent, which were alleged to constitute the proceeds of criminal activity. The respondent contested the order on the grounds that the Mareva-type jurisdiction was limited to civil debt claims and could not be deployed in the proceeds of crime context.

The High Court rejected this argument and granted the freezing order. The court confirmed that the Mareva/freezing jurisdiction, as recognised in Ireland in Caudron v Air Zaire [1985] IR 716, is grounded in the court's inherent power to prevent its processes from being rendered futile. That power is not confined to any particular category of civil proceedings but applies wherever there is a real risk that assets will be removed or dissipated in a manner that would undermine the effective administration of justice.

The court noted that the Proceeds of Crime Act 1996 itself provides a specific statutory mechanism for asset freezing in criminal proceedings, but that the inherent equitable jurisdiction provides a complementary and flexible tool available to the court in appropriate circumstances.

This decision illustrates the ongoing development of the Irish freezing jurisdiction and its application across a broad range of proceedings, including those engaged with the criminal justice system's asset recovery mechanisms. It demonstrates that the equitable principles underlying Mareva relief are not rigidly confined to their original commercial context.

legal_principle: Where a trustee or fiduciary mixes trust money with their own money in a bank account and subsequently makes withdrawals, equity presumes that the trustee spent their own money first and that the trust money remains in the account to the extent that the balance permits. This principle — sometimes called the lowest intermediate balance rule — allows the beneficiary to trace and assert a proprietary claim to the remaining balance in the mixed account. The rule reflects equity's maxim that a trustee shall not benefit from their own wrongdoing at the expense of the beneficiary.
key_quote: The moment you find a trustee has mixed money with his own, the cestui que trust has a charge on that mixed fund for the amount of the trust money, and the trustee must be taken to have withdrawn his own money first.
full_summary:
Hallett was a solicitor who held money on trust for clients and also received money from Mrs Cotterill under a transaction that created a fiduciary obligation. He paid these funds into his own bank account, thereby mixing them with his own money. He subsequently made various withdrawals, reducing the account balance at certain points. On his death and insolvency, the question arose as to whether the beneficiaries could trace their money in the mixed account.

The Court of Appeal held that the beneficiaries were entitled to trace equitably into the mixed account. Jessel MR articulated the central principle: where a trustee mixes trust money with their own, equity treats the withdrawals as having been made from the trustee's own money first. The trust money is therefore presumed to remain in the account for as long as the balance is sufficient.

This rule serves to protect innocent beneficiaries against the consequences of a trustee's breach of duty. It reflects the maxim that a wrongdoer cannot take advantage of their own wrong: if the trustee's conduct in mixing funds is itself a breach of trust, equity will not allow the trustee to claim priority in the mixed fund at the expense of the beneficiary.

Re Hallett's Estate is the foundational authority on equitable tracing into mixed bank accounts. Its presumption was subsequently qualified and extended in Re Oatway [1903] 2 Ch 356, and remains the starting point for any analysis of equitable tracing through a commingled fund.

legal_principle: Where a trustee mixes trust money with their own funds and then makes a profitable investment from the mixed account before dissipating the remaining balance, the beneficiary is entitled to trace into and claim the investment rather than being confined to the diminished remainder. Re Hallett's Estate is modified: the presumption that the trustee spends their own money first cannot be used to allow the trustee to appropriate a profitable asset while leaving the beneficiary with nothing. Equity will not permit the trustee to elect which part of the fund was 'theirs' so as to defeat the beneficiary's claim.
key_quote: It is settled that when a trustee has mixed trust money with his own and afterwards makes a purchase, the cestui que trust is entitled to claim the purchase as part of the trust property, or to have a charge upon it.
full_summary:
Oatway was a trustee who paid trust money into his own bank account, mixing it with his own funds. He then used money from the mixed account to purchase shares, which proved profitable. Thereafter, he spent the remaining balance in the account on his own affairs, so that by the time of his death the account was exhausted. The question was whether the beneficiaries could claim the shares.

Joyce J held that the beneficiaries were entitled to trace into the shares. He distinguished the situation from the simple application of Re Hallett's principle. In Re Hallett, the trustee had only made withdrawals that could be attributed to his own funds, leaving trust money intact. In Re Oatway, if the Re Hallett presumption were applied mechanically — treating the purchase of shares as made from the trustee's own money — the trustee would be left with a profitable asset and the beneficiary with nothing. That outcome would be unconscionable.

Joyce J held that the trust property is to be identified so as to give the beneficiary the best possible result. If the trustee invests from the mixed fund and then dissipates the balance, the beneficiary may elect to claim the investment as trust property. The trustee cannot by selecting which withdrawals to treat as 'their own' engineer a result in which they retain the benefit of the trust money.

Re Oatway is therefore an important qualification to Re Hallett and together the two cases establish the flexible approach to tracing in mixed accounts: the court will apply whichever rule best protects the beneficiary's position, subject to the overall constraint of the lowest intermediate balance.

legal_principle: Tracing is a process by which a claimant identifies a substituted asset as the product of their original asset; it is not itself a remedy but a preliminary step to the assertion of a proprietary claim. Where trust money is mixed with the trustee's own funds and used to purchase an asset, the beneficiaries are entitled to claim a proportionate share of that asset, including any profit it has generated. A proprietary claim survives the trustee's insolvency and gives the beneficiary priority over unsecured creditors, which is the principal advantage of tracing in equity over a personal claim.
key_quote: Tracing is thus neither a claim nor a remedy. It is merely the process by which a claimant demonstrates what has happened to his property, identifies its proceeds and the persons who have handled or received them, and justifies his claim that the proceeds can be treated as representing his property.
full_summary:
Murphy was a trustee of a trust fund used to purchase land in Portugal. He misappropriated money from the trust fund and used it, together with his own premiums, to pay insurance premiums on a life insurance policy. On Murphy's death, the policy paid out a substantial sum (approximately £1 million) to his children. The beneficiaries of the trust fund claimed entitlement to a proportionate share of the insurance proceeds, tracing their money through the premium payments.

The House of Lords, by a majority, held in favour of the beneficiaries. Lord Millett delivered the leading speech, in which he drew a fundamental distinction between tracing (the evidential process of identifying property and its substitutes) and the proprietary claim that follows a successful tracing exercise. Tracing is a process; it does not of itself give rise to a remedy but enables the claimant to identify property as representing their original asset.
